.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:1383:"C:\\Program Files\\PowerShell\\7\\pwsh.exe" -Command 'Get-Content -Raw outputs/sweep_r3_final/due_diligence/dd_lilly_tirzepatide_manufacturing/report.md' in C:\POLARIS
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:1488:# Research report: What is the current state of Eli Lilly's tirzepatide manufacturing capacity and supply outlook?
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:1492:Eli Lilly plans a $200 million expansion at its Suzhou, China manufacturing site to produce tirzepatide medications, which will add 120 new positions and supply both China and Europe.[1] The company is undertaking massive global manufacturing investments to meet demand, including a $9 billion plant in Lebanon, Indiana for tirzepatide active pharmaceutical ingredients (API), which it describes as the single largest investment in synthetic medicine API manufacturing in U.S. history.[2][3] This Indiana site investment was increased by an additional $5.3 billion, bringing the total there to $9 billion.[4][2] Lilly also announced a $3 billion investment to expand a manufacturing plant in Pleasant Prairie, Wisconsin, which it characterizes as its single largest U.S. manufacturing investment outside Indiana.[5][4] The Wisconsin expansion is expected to create 750 new jobs and follows the acquisition of that facility from Nexus Pharmaceuticals.[4][3] Since 2020, the company has committed a total of more than $23 billion to building, expanding, and buying manufacturing sites.[5][4] One source reports the total for new U.S. and European sites since 2020 as $16 billion, while another states the overall manufacturing investments for making Zepbound and Mounjaro total more than $18 billion.[2][3]
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:1496:The market demand for tirzepatide is exceptionally strong, with its franchise becoming the world’s best-selling drug in the third quarter, bringing in a combined $10.1 billion.[6] This demand shows few signs of slowing, as earnings growth has been driven by an increase in the volume of GLP-1 drugs.[7] Eli Lilly gained market share for the fifth consecutive quarter, with its drugs accounting for nearly 6 out of 10 prescriptions within the injectable obesity and diabetes class.[8] Analysts project massive future sales, with Mounjaro sales expected to reach $18.4 billion in 2025 and $22.8 billion in 2026, while Zepbound is projected to jump from $4.9 billion in 2024 to $12.5 billion in 2025 and $18.1 billion in 2026.[7] The overall market potential is vast, with market penetration for all GLP-1 drugs estimated at only 4% of a target audience of 100 to 120 million people in the USA alone.[7]
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:1500:The global incretin market, which includes GLP-1s, is forecast to reach $200 billion by 2030.[9] Morgan Global Research estimates that approximately 25 million Americans will be on GLP-1 treatment by 2030, up from around 10 million in 2025, 6 million in 2024 and 5 million in 2023.[9] This demand is driven by volume growth for drugs like tirzepatide, as evidenced by Lilly's Q4 2024 revenue increasing 45% to $13.53 billion driven by volume growth from Mounjaro and Zepbound.[10] To address supply constraints, Eli Lilly has launched a multi-state, multi-year manufacturing expansion driven by unprecedented demand for its GLP-1 diabetes and obesity therapies.[11] This includes doubling its investment at its LEAP campus in Indiana to $9 billion to boost active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) output for tirzepatide.[11] The company has also added a $4.5 billion “Medicine Foundry” campus in Lebanon and expanded a greenfield plant in North Carolina from $1 billion to $2 billion for injectable medicines and drug-device assembly.[11] This build-out is part of a broader plan involving multiple new plants to scale production.[11]
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:1520:[1] Lilly plans $200M expansion in China to make tirzepatide meds — https://www.fiercepharma.com/manufacturing/lillys-manufacturing-splurge-continues-200m-expansion-china (tier T6)
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:1523:[4] Lilly Pumps $3B Into Wisconsin Site as Compounders Close In on ... — https://www.biospace.com/business/lilly-pumps-3b-into-wisconsin-site-as-compounders-close-in-on-tirzepatide (tier T4)
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:1542:Novo Nordisk launched an oral version of its weight-loss drug Wegovy, priced at $149 a month for the 1.5 mg and 4 mg doses, while higher 9 mg and 25 mg doses cost $299 per month.[6] The company stated the 4 mg dose would remain at $149 through April 15 before rising to $199, a move aimed at easing patient access during rollout.[6] This semaglutide pill won FDA approval in December for weight management and lowering cardiovascular risk.[6] The launch helps Novo defend share in the oral obesity market as Eli Lilly plans to price its competing pill, orforglipron, at $149 for the entry dose if approved early in 2026.[6] A real-world study suggested continued use of Wegovy led to a 57% greater reduction in the risk of heart attack, stroke, or death compared with users of tirzepatide, the active ingredient in Eli Lilly's Zepbound, though the number of patients recording such events was very low at 0.1% for Wegovy and 0.4% for tirzepatide.[7] The company noted these results came from a real-world study as opposed to a randomized control trial and said the study added to evidence that the heart-protective benefits are specific to semaglutide.[7] In a comparative 72-week study of adults without diabetes, participants on the maximum tolerated dose of Zepbound (10 mg or 15 mg) experienced on average a 20.2% weight loss compared to an average of 13.7% weight loss for participants on the maximum tolerated dose of Wegovy (1.7 mg or 2.4 mg) at the time of the study.[8]
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:1550:Novo Nordisk's long-term market outlook faces significant headwinds from intense competition and pricing pressure, with its 2026 sales forecast to fall between 5% and 13% after years of double-digit gains.[13] This sharp projected drop is attributed to aggressive U.S. price cuts, rising competition, and the loss of important patent protections for semaglutide.[13] In contrast, its main competitor Eli Lilly forecast stronger-than-expected growth for 2026, estimating sales between $80 billion and $83 billion.[13] The broader GLP-1 market, however, is projected for substantial long-term expansion, with the global market for these drugs expected to reach USD 324.5 billion by 2035, reflecting a compound annual growth rate of 17.3% from 2024.[14] Specifically for Wegovy, the market size is estimated at USD 1.95 billion in 2025 and is expected to reach USD 2.73 billion by 2030, at a CAGR of greater than 7%.[15] Novo Nordisk's current strong position is evidenced by its 69% global share in the GLP-1 market as of Q2 2024 and the fact that its key drug Ozempic represented 37.3% of GLP-1 sales by July 2025.[14] However, the sustainability of this dominance is challenged by competitors like Lilly's tirzepatide, which has demonstrated superior weight loss efficacy compared to Novo Nordisk's offerings.[14] .[14] The company's market capitalization also decreased from its 2024 peak, reaching $246.82 billion USD by July 2025.[14]

.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:4319:"C:\\Program Files\\PowerShell\\7\\pwsh.exe" -Command 'Get-Content -Raw outputs/sweep_r3_final/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md' in C:\POLARIS
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:4327:# Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:4331:The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] One meta-analysis reported a weighted mean difference in HbA1c of -1.07% with tirzepatide treatment.[2] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the completed SURPASS trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] In the SURPASS trials, body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine (SURPASS-5) to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor (SURPASS-3).[1] Another meta-analysis reported a weighted mean difference in body weight of -7.99 kg.[2] The proportion of participants achieving weight loss of ≥15% in SURPASS-2, for example, was 15%, 24%, and 36% for tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg, respectively, compared to 8% for semaglutide 1 mg.[3] Tirzepatide was superior to active comparators including semaglutide 1 mg and insulin degludec in reducing both A1c and body weight.[1]
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:4335:In a phase 3b trial comparing tirzepatide and semaglutide in adults with obesity but without type 2 diabetes, tirzepatide demonstrated superior weight loss efficacy.[4] The least-squares mean percent change in weight at week 72 was −20.2% with tirzepatide compared to −13.7% with semaglutide (P<0.001).[4] Tirzepatide also resulted in a greater reduction in waist circumference, with a least-squares mean change of −18.4 cm versus −13.0 cm with semaglutide (P<0.001).[4] Participants receiving tirzepatide were more likely than those receiving semaglutide to achieve weight reductions of at least 10%, 15%, 20%, and 25%.[4] The most common adverse events in both treatment groups were gastrointestinal, and most were mild to moderate in severity.[4]
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:4339:In a trial of adults with obesity and type 2 diabetes, body weight reduction at week 72 was significantly greater with tirzepatide versus placebo, with reductions of -12.8% (SE 0.6) for the 10 mg dose and -14.7% (SE 0.5) for the 15 mg dose, compared to -3.2% (SE 0.5) for placebo.[5] Hemoglobin A1c was also significantly reduced with tirzepatide versus placebo after 72 weeks, with reductions of -2.1% for both the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to -0.5% for placebo.[5] For the maintenance of weight loss specifically, the SURMOUNT-4 trial investigated continued treatment after an initial 36-week lead-in period where participants achieved a mean weight reduction of 20.9%.[6] At week 88, 89.5% of participants continuing tirzepatide maintained at least 80% of their initial weight loss, compared to 16.6% of those on placebo.[6] Continued tirzepatide treatment also reduced the risk of returning to greater than 95% of baseline body weight by approximately 98% compared with placebo.[6] In a separate trial following a lifestyle intervention lead-in, participants taking tirzepatide achieved an additional 21.1% mean weight loss, for a total mean weight loss of 26.6% from study entry to 84 weeks.[7]
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:4347:Limitations: The corpus has a notable gap in high-tier evidence, with only 25% of sources being T1 primary studies, while 50% are T7 (news/media). The pipeline detected 2 high-severity contradictions among sources, specifically on the magnitude of effect for tirzepatide on body weight (10 mg) and on weight loss (15 mg). The evidence horizon spans from 2010 to the present, but the heavy reliance on lower-tier sources may limit the robustness of the synthesized conclusions.
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:4364:- tirzepatide / body weight (10 mg): values [-83.0, 5.0] %, rel diff 106.0%.
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:4365:- tirzepatide / weight loss (15 mg): values [7.0, 20.9] %, rel diff 198.6%.
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:4370:[2] Efficacy and safety of tirzepatide in patients with type 2 ... — https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full (tier T1)
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:4373:[5] Once-weekly tirzepatide significantly improves weight and ... — https://www.2minutemedicine.com/once-weekly-tirzepatide-significantly-improves-weight-and-glycemic-control-in-patients-with-with-obesity-and-type-2-diabetes/ (tier T4)
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:4375:[7] Tirzepatide trial shows additional 21.1% weight loss following ... — https://news.vumc.org/2023/10/17/tirzepatide-trial-shows-additional-21-1-weight-loss-following-intensive-lifestyle-intervention/ (tier T4)

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\codex_stderr.log:256:                FAIL PT08: Missing disclosures for: ['tirzepatide/body weight (10 mg)', 'tirzepatide/body weight (15 mg)', 'tir
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\codex_stderr.log:302:# Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\codex_stderr.log:306:Across the SURPASS program, a high proportion of participants achieved A1c targets, with 81% to 97% achieving an A1c <7.0%, 66% to 95% achieving ≤6.5%, and 23% to 62% achieving normoglycemia (A1c <5.7%) across the different doses and trials.[1] In that trial, the proportions of patients achieving an A1c <7.0% were 75.4%, 86.0%, and 84.4% with the three tirzepatide doses, respectively, versus 23.7% with insulin glargine.[2] Tirzepatide also produced significant, dose-dependent weight loss across the type 2 diabetes trials.[1] A systematic review notes that across phase 3 trials, tirzepatide achieved greater reductions in hemoglobin A1c (up to 2.4%) and body weight (up to 22%) compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[3] Beyond glycemic and weight control, tirzepatide improved cardiometabolic parameters across the SURPASS trials, including reductions in fasting serum glucose, systolic and diastolic blood pressure, triglycerides, LDL cholesterol, VLDL cholesterol, and increases in HDL cholesterol.[1] An individual participant data meta-analysis of phase 3 trials reported that tirzepatide 15 mg/week significantly reduced the risk of major adverse cardiovascular events compared to comparators, with a hazard ratio of 0.67 (95% CI, 0.51-0.87).[4]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\codex_stderr.log:310:The most frequent adverse events associated with tirzepatide are gastrointestinal in nature, with nausea, diarrhea, and vomiting being the most common and typically mild to moderate in severity.[5] In the SURPASS Phase 3 clinical trials for type 2 diabetes, the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events was dose-dependent, with nausea reported in 20–33% of participants, diarrhea in 18–22%, and vomiting in 8–13%.[5] A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials in patients with diabetes and/or obesity similarly reports that gastrointestinal effects are the most common adverse events.[6] In a retrospective study of adults with type 1 diabetes and obesity, the overall incidence of side effects was 26%, with nausea being the most common at 15%, and only 4% of patients discontinued tirzepatide due to side effects.[7] In the SURMOUNT-1 trial for obesity, the vast majority of participants (90%) who were initially late responders (achieving <5% weight loss at week 12) went on to achieve clinically meaningful weight reduction by week 72, suggesting a generally tolerable profile that allows for continued treatment.[8] Serious adverse events appear uncommon; in a Phase I trial of a bispecific GLP-1/GLP-2 agonist, no serious adverse events or discontinuations due to adverse events occurred, and treatment-emergent adverse events were mostly mild to moderate gastrointestinal events.[9] Furthermore, in the retrospective study of patients with type 1 diabetes, no episodes of severe hypoglycemia or diabetic ketoacidosis were recorded during tirzepatide treatment.[7]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\codex_stderr.log:314:Compared with insulin glargine in the SURPASS-4 trial, tirzepatide at 104 weeks led to significantly greater reductions in HbA1c (5 mg: -2.3%, 10 mg: -2.5%, 15 mg: -2.6% vs. -1.0% with insulin glargine) and body weight (5 mg: -7.6 kg, 10 mg: -10.0 kg, 15 mg: -11.4 kg vs. +2.1 kg with insulin glargine).[10] A meta-analysis of six RCTs found tirzepatide reduced body weight more than the GLP-1 receptor agonists semaglutide 1 mg and dulaglutide 1.5 mg, and resulted in a higher proportion of patients achieving weight loss thresholds of ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15%.[11] In the head-to-head SURPASS-2 trial, all three tirzepatide doses (5, 10, and 15 mg) demonstrated superior HbA1c and weight reductions compared to once-weekly semaglutide 1 mg at 40 weeks.[12] A model-based simulation of switching from stable GLP-1 RA therapy predicted that transitioning to tirzepatide could yield further HbA1c reductions of 1.95% to 2.46% and body weight reductions of 6.50 to 12.1 kg by week 66.[13] For weight maintenance after initial loss, the SURMOUNT-4 trial in adults with obesity (without diabetes) showed that continuing tirzepatide led to an additional 5.5% weight reduction from weeks 36 to 88, whereas switching to placebo resulted in a 14.0% weight regain over the same period.[14] By week 88, 69.5% of participants continuing tirzepatide achieved ≥20% weight loss from baseline, compared to 12.6% in the placebo group.[14] In a related obesity trial without diabetes, SURMOUNT-1, tirzepatide 15 mg produced a mean weight reduction of 20.9% at 72 weeks versus 3.1% with placebo.[15] Regarding safety, the meta-analysis reported that tirzepatide did not increase the risk of hypoglycemia compared to semaglutide 1 mg.[11] Gastrointestinal adverse events, such as nausea, diarrhea, and vomiting, were more common with tirzepatide than placebo but were predominantly mild to moderate in severity.[14] In SURMOUNT-4, treatment discontinuation due to an adverse event during the double-blind period occurred in 1.8% of the tirzepatide group and 0.9% of the placebo group.[14] A post-hoc analysis of SURMOUNT trials found that adverse events potentially related to macronutrient malnutrition, such as abnormal weight loss or underweight, were uncommon, occurring in 0.12% of tirzepatide-treated participants.[16]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\codex_stderr.log:318:Limitations: The evidence base is heavily weighted toward lower-tier sources, with only 20% of sources classified as T1 primary studies. High-severity contradictions were detected on the effect of tirzepatide on body weight and weight loss, with sources disagreeing on the magnitude of the effect by several hundred to several thousand percent. The corpus spans from 2010 to the present, but the low proportion of high-tier evidence limits the strength of conclusions that can be drawn.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:263:  "question": "What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:282:  "run_id": "SWEEP_clinical_clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm_1776629105",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:283:  "slug": "clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:289:# Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:293:In the pivotal SURPASS phase 3 trials, all doses of tirzepatide demonstrated superiority in reducing A1C and body weight from baseline versus placebo or active comparators in adults with type 2 diabetes.[1] The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] In SURPASS-2, which compared tirzepatide to semaglutide 1 mg, reductions in A1C at week 40 were −2.09%, −2.37%, and −2.46% for tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg, respectively, versus −1.86% for semaglutide.[1] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the completed SURPASS trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] Body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine in SURPASS-5 to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor in SURPASS-3.[1] In SURPASS-6, adding tirzepatide to insulin glargine resulted in an estimated mean change from baseline in HbA1c of −2.1% versus −1.1% with added prandial insulin lispro at week 52.[2] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial in the Asia-Pacific region, all tirzepatide doses were superior to insulin glargine for HbA1c reduction, with reductions of −2.24%, −2.44%, and −2.49% for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus −0.95% for insulin glargine at week 40.[3] In the same trial, body weight reductions were −5.0 kg, −7.0 kg, and −7.2 kg for the respective tirzepatide doses compared to a 1.5 kg increase with insulin glargine.[3]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:297:An indirect treatment comparison in people with obesity or overweight and type 2 diabetes estimated that tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg were associated with significantly greater mean percent weight reductions versus semaglutide 2.4 mg, with mean differences of 2.57% and 4.79%, respectively.[4] A separate systematic review and meta-analysis of direct comparative studies concluded tirzepatide was significantly superior to semaglutide in achieving weight reduction, with a pooled standardized mean difference of 0.75 (95% CI: 0.52 to 0.92).[5] A network meta-analysis in patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg showed statistically significant, greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight versus the GLP-1 receptor agonists dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[6]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:301:In randomized controlled trials, the overall safety profile of tirzepatide is similar to that of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs), with gastrointestinal adverse events being the most common.[7] A systematic review of RCTs found the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events with tirzepatide was consistent with GLP-1RAs.[8] The most frequently reported gastrointestinal events in a real-world pharmacovigilance analysis were nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea.[9] In a related population of patients with obesity or overweight, a network meta-analysis found tirzepatide showed a higher severe hypoglycemia risk (<54 mg/dL) compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[10] Real-world data also indicate hypoglycemia risk may be heightened by concomitant use of other hypoglycemic drugs.[9] A post-marketing surveillance analysis detected disproportionate reporting of gastrointestinal events, such as nausea (ROR 4.01, 95% CI 3.85–4.19), but concluded tirzepatide exhibited a similar risk of gastrointestinal adverse events compared to GLP-1RAs.[7]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:305:A network meta-analysis found that compared to the 5 mg dose, tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg provided significantly greater reductions in HbA1c (mean differences of -0.19% and -0.32%, respectively) and body weight (mean differences of -1.96 kg and -3.31 kg, respectively).[11] A separate analysis confirmed that tirzepatide 15 mg significantly reduced HbA1c by 0.30% and weight by 4.26 kg compared to the 5 mg dose.[12] This dose-response pattern for weight loss is also observed in a related obesity trial without diabetes, where higher tirzepatide doses (10 mg and 15 mg) were associated with higher proportions of participants achieving various weight reduction thresholds and greater mean percent weight reductions at 72 weeks compared to the 5 mg dose.[13] Regarding the time to achieve treatment goals, a pre-planned analysis of SURPASS trials reported that the median time to reach an HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all three tirzepatide doses, while the median time to first achieve ≥5% weight loss was 16.0 weeks for the 5 mg dose and 12.4 weeks for the 10 and 15 mg doses.[14] However, the increased efficacy of higher doses is accompanied by a higher risk of adverse events, particularly gastrointestinal events, with one analysis noting a significantly higher risk of any adverse event for the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[12] Consequently, one review concluded that while tirzepatide 15 mg provides the best efficacy for glycemic and weight reduction, the 5 mg dose offers a more favorable safety profile.[15]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:309:Tirzepatide is a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist.[16] A phase 1 clinical trial specifically assessed the effects of subcutaneous tirzepatide versus placebo or semaglutide on pancreatic islet function and insulin sensitivity in adults with type 2 diabetes.[17] A systematic review describes that the dual incretin agonism of tirzepatide enhances insulin secretion in a glucose-dependent manner and improves insulin sensitivity.[18] In a substudy of the SURPASS-3 trial, tirzepatide was shown to reduce liver fat content and abdominal adipose tissue in people with type 2 diabetes compared with insulin degludec.[19] The combined infusion of GIP and GLP-1 is noted to produce a more pronounced glucose-lowering effect and enhanced insulin secretion than the administration of either agent alone.[16] The reduction in liver fat and visceral adipose tissue is a mechanism by which tirzepatide may improve systemic insulin sensitivity.[19]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:313:Limitations: The corpus has significant tier-distribution gaps, with only 15% of sources being T1 primary studies and a heavy reliance on T4 (26%) and T7 (31%) material. The pipeline detected 12 contradictions, all concerning tirzepatide's effect on body weight or weight loss, where sources disagree substantially on the magnitude of the reported outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010 to the present, but the concentration of lower-tier sources and high-severity contradictions indicates potential reliability issues in the aggregated data.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:679:    "url": "https://www.emjreviews.com/oncology/news/investigating-fda-reports-of-tirzepatide-thyroid-cancer-connection/"
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:684:    "statement": "Optimal dose of tirzepatide for type 2 diabetes mellitus: A meta-analysis and trial sequential analysis",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:741:# Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:745:Tirzepatide demonstrated significant, dose-dependent improvements in both glycemic control and body weight across the SURPASS clinical trials in adults with type 2 diabetes.[1] The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] In SURPASS-1, the highest dose (15 mg) reduced A1C by 2.07% from baseline and 52% of participants achieved normoglycemia (A1c <5.7%).[1][2] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] For weight loss, body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine (SURPASS-5) to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor (SURPASS-3).[1] Tirzepatide was superior to active comparators, including semaglutide 1 mg and various insulin regimens, in reducing A1C and body weight.[1][3] These effects were consistent across different patient populations and background therapies.[1][4]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:749:In a direct head-to-head trial in patients with obesity without diabetes, tirzepatide demonstrated superior weight loss compared to semaglutide, with a least-squares mean percent change in weight at week 72 of −20.2% versus −13.7%.[5] Participants were also more likely to achieve weight reductions of at least 10%, 15%, 20%, and 25% with tirzepatide.[5] Another phase 3 trial reported a mean percentage change in body weight with tirzepatide of −20.2% compared with −13.7% for semaglutide at 72 weeks.[6] A real-world retrospective cohort study in patients without diabetes also found greater 6-month mean percentage weight reduction with tirzepatide (−11.15%) versus semaglutide (−8.83%).[7] A systematic review and meta-analysis of direct comparative studies concluded tirzepatide was significantly superior to semaglutide in achieving weight reduction, with a greater standardized mean difference for weight loss.[8] For glycemic control in type 2 diabetes, a network meta-analysis found all tirzepatide doses (5, 10, 15 mg) were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c.[9] An indirect treatment comparison also concluded tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg were associated with significantly greater mean percent weight reductions versus semaglutide 2.4 mg in people with obesity and type 2 diabetes.[10] Compared to insulin, tirzepatide showed greater efficacy; a meta-analysis found once-weekly tirzepatide significantly decreased HbA1c and body weight compared with daily insulin glargine and degludec, with a lower risk of hypoglycemia.[11] Specifically, in a trial adding therapy to basal insulin, the mean change in HbA1c at week 52 was −2.1% with tirzepatide versus −1.1% with insulin lispro, with less hypoglycemia and more body weight reduction.[12] Another meta-analysis reported all three tirzepatide doses resulted in a significant reduction in HbA1c and a significant decrease in body weight compared with long-acting insulin.[13] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal events were the most common adverse events for both tirzepatide and semaglutide in the head-to-head obesity trial, with one source reporting at least one adverse event in approximately 76.7% of patients on tirzepatide and 79% on semaglutide.[5][6] A network meta-analysis in patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin found tirzepatide showed a statistically significant, higher likelihood of experiencing nausea compared with placebo or exenatide, but no significant difference in treatment discontinuation due to adverse events compared to several GLP-1 receptor agonists.[14]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:753:Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most commonly reported adverse events with tirzepatide and are dose-dependent, with reported incidences of 39% (95% CI, 35%-43%), 46% (95% CI, 42%-49%), and 49% (95% CI, 38%-60%) for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively.[15] Among gastrointestinal events, nausea and diarrhea are the most frequent at any dose.[15] Drug discontinuation due to adverse events was highest with the 15 mg dose at 10%.[15] Regarding hypoglycemia, a meta-analysis found patients treated with tirzepatide had no significantly increased risk compared with placebo overall (OR= 2.05, 95%CI:0.95 to 4.42, p=0.07).[16] Another analysis reported tirzepatide 15mg was associated with more hypoglycemia than GLP-1 receptor agonists (pooled RR=3.83, 95% CI [1.19- 12.30], P=0.02).[17] Conversely, a different study in patients achieving normoglycemia found low rates, with 98.5% not experiencing level 2 or 3 severe hypoglycemia (<54 mg/dL) and 91% not experiencing level 1 hypoglycemia (≤70 mg/dL).[18] Rates of fatal adverse events, severe hypoglycemia, acute pancreatitis, cholelithiasis, and cholecystitis are reported as extremely low (≤ 1%) across all doses.[15] An analysis of FDA adverse event reports suggested an association between tirzepatide and thyroid cancer (reporting odds ratio = 2.09, 95% CI: 1.51–2.89), though this was lower than associations for other GLP-1 receptor agonists.[19]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:757:The effects of tirzepatide on glycemic and weight outcomes are dose-dependent within the 5 mg to 15 mg range.[20] This analysis also noted that fasting serum glucose showed improvement with the 15 mg dose.[21] One review concluded that within the 5–15 mg dose range, the effect in lowering glycemia and reducing weight is dose-dependent, with the 15 mg dose achieving the best efficacy benefit.[20] A separate meta-analysis confirmed that the 15 mg dose provided the greatest reductions in HbA1c and body weight compared to the 10 mg and 5 mg doses.[22] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal adverse events increased with higher doses, though one analysis reported this increase was without statistical significance.[21] Another analysis found the risk of total adverse events was significantly higher with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[22]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:761:In a post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-1 to -5, tirzepatide demonstrated significantly greater reductions in HbA1c versus comparators in both prespecified age subgroups (<65 and ≥65 years).[23] Regarding sex, all tirzepatide doses significantly reduced body weight compared to comparators regardless of the sex of study participants across SURPASS-1 to -5.[24] In patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and obesity, tirzepatide produced less weight loss at 52 weeks in those with type 2 diabetes (10.4%) than in those without diabetes (12.9%).[25] However, the effect of tirzepatide on the primary composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure was similar between patients with or without diabetes, with a hazard ratio of 0.64 for patients with diabetes and 0.61 for those without.[25] In a retrospective study of adults with type 1 diabetes and obesity, tirzepatide led to significant weight loss, with a median total body weight loss of 8% at 6 months and a 1% reduction in HbA1c.[26] For individuals with type 2 diabetes who were glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibody-positive, tirzepatide was effective, achieving HbA1c reductions of -2.11% and body weight reductions of -9.2 kg at 40/42 weeks, though HbA1c reductions were slightly greater in GADA-negative patients.[27] The most frequent adverse events across subgroups were mild-to-moderate and gastrointestinal-related, occurring during dose-escalation.[23][24]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:765:Limitations: The corpus exhibits significant tier-distribution gaps, with only 15% of sources being T1 primary studies and a heavy reliance on T7 sources at 32%. The pipeline detected 11 high-severity contradictions, primarily concerning tirzepatide's effect on body weight, where sources disagree substantially on the magnitude of the reported outcome. The evidence horizon spans from 2010 to the present, which may not capture earlier foundational research.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1350:2424:    "title": "Efficacy of once-weekly tirzepatide versus once-daily insulin degludec on glycaemic control measured by continuous glucose monitoring in adults with type 2 diabetes (SURPASS-3 CGM): a substudy of the randomised, open-label, parallel-group, phase 3 SURPASS-3 trial.",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1357:outputs/full_scale_v5/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:5:Tirzepatide demonstrated significant, dose-dependent improvements in both glycemic control and body weight across the SURPASS clinical trials in adults with type 2 diabetes.[1] The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] In SURPASS-1, the highest dose (15 mg) reduced A1C by 2.07% from baseline and 52% of participants achieved normoglycemia (A1c <5.7%).[1][2] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] For weight loss, body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine (SURPASS-5) to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor (SURPASS-3).[1] Tirzepatide was superior to active comparators, including semaglutide 1 mg and various insulin regimens, in reducing A1C and body weight.[1][3] These effects were consistent across different patient populations and background therapies.[1][4]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1358:outputs/full_scale_v5/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:21:In a post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-1 to -5, tirzepatide demonstrated significantly greater reductions in HbA1c versus comparators in both prespecified age subgroups (<65 and ≥65 years).[23] Regarding sex, all tirzepatide doses significantly reduced body weight compared to comparators regardless of the sex of study participants across SURPASS-1 to -5.[24] In patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and obesity, tirzepatide produced less weight loss at 52 weeks in those with type 2 diabetes (10.4%) than in those without diabetes (12.9%).[25] However, the effect of tirzepatide on the primary composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure was similar between patients with or without diabetes, with a hazard ratio of 0.64 for patients with diabetes and 0.61 for those without.[25] In a retrospective study of adults with type 1 diabetes and obesity, tirzepatide led to significant weight loss, with a median total body weight loss of 8% at 6 months and a 1% reduction in HbA1c.[26] For individuals with type 2 diabetes who were glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibody-positive, tirzepatide was effective, achieving HbA1c reductions of -2.11% and body weight reductions of -9.2 kg at 40/42 weeks, though HbA1c reductions were slightly greater in GADA-negative patients.[27] The most frequent adverse events across subgroups were mild-to-moderate and gastrointestinal-related, occurring during dose-escalation.[23][24]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1367:outputs/full_scale_v5/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json:26:    "statement": "227-OR: Tirzepatide Reduces HbA1c and Body Weight Significantly More than Placebo Regardless of Insulin Sensitivity and Beta-Cell Function\u2014Post Hoc Analysis from SURMOUNT-2",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1368:outputs/full_scale_v5/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json:82:    "statement": "Tirzepatide vs Insulin Lispro Added to Basal Insulin in Type 2 Diabetes: The SURPASS-6 Randomized Clinical Trial.",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1369:outputs/full_scale_v6/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:5:In the pivotal SURPASS phase 3 trials, all doses of tirzepatide demonstrated superiority in reducing A1C and body weight from baseline versus placebo or active comparators in adults with type 2 diabetes.[1] The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] In SURPASS-2, which compared tirzepatide to semaglutide 1 mg, reductions in A1C at week 40 were −2.09%, −2.37%, and −2.46% for tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg, respectively, versus −1.86% for semaglutide.[1] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the completed SURPASS trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] Body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine in SURPASS-5 to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor in SURPASS-3.[1] In SURPASS-6, adding tirzepatide to insulin glargine resulted in an estimated mean change from baseline in HbA1c of −2.1% versus −1.1% with added prandial insulin lispro at week 52.[2] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial in the Asia-Pacific region, all tirzepatide doses were superior to insulin glargine for HbA1c reduction, with reductions of −2.24%, −2.44%, and −2.49% for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus −0.95% for insulin glargine at week 40.[3] In the same trial, body weight reductions were −5.0 kg, −7.0 kg, and −7.2 kg for the respective tirzepatide doses compared to a 1.5 kg increase with insulin glargine.[3]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1370:outputs/full_scale_v6/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:21:Tirzepatide is a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist.[16] A phase 1 clinical trial specifically assessed the effects of subcutaneous tirzepatide versus placebo or semaglutide on pancreatic islet function and insulin sensitivity in adults with type 2 diabetes.[17] A systematic review describes that the dual incretin agonism of tirzepatide enhances insulin secretion in a glucose-dependent manner and improves insulin sensitivity.[18] In a substudy of the SURPASS-3 trial, tirzepatide was shown to reduce liver fat content and abdominal adipose tissue in people with type 2 diabetes compared with insulin degludec.[19] The combined infusion of GIP and GLP-1 is noted to produce a more pronounced glucose-lowering effect and enhanced insulin secretion than the administration of either agent alone.[16] The reduction in liver fat and visceral adipose tissue is a mechanism by which tirzepatide may improve systemic insulin sensitivity.[19]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:2636:  1: # Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:2640:  5: In the pivotal SURPASS phase 3 trials, all doses of tirzepatide demonstrated superiority in reducing A1C and body weight from baseline versus placebo or active comparators in adults with type 2 diabetes.[1] The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] In SURPASS-2, which compared tirzepatide to semaglutide 1 mg, reductions in A1C at week 40 were −2.09%, −2.37%, and −2.46% for tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg, respectively, versus −1.86% for semaglutide.[1] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the completed SURPASS trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] Body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine in SURPASS-5 to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor in SURPASS-3.[1] In SURPASS-6, adding tirzepatide to insulin glargine resulted in an estimated mean change from baseline in HbA1c of −2.1% versus −1.1% with added prandial insulin lispro at week 52.[2] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial in the Asia-Pacific region, all tirzepatide doses were superior to insulin glargine for HbA1c reduction, with reductions of −2.24%, −2.44%, and −2.49% for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus −0.95% for insulin glargine at week 40.[3] In the same trial, body weight reductions were −5.0 kg, −7.0 kg, and −7.2 kg for the respective tirzepatide doses compared to a 1.5 kg increase with insulin glargine.[3]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:2644:  9: An indirect treatment comparison in people with obesity or overweight and type 2 diabetes estimated that tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg were associated with significantly greater mean percent weight reductions versus semaglutide 2.4 mg, with mean differences of 2.57% and 4.79%, respectively.[4] A separate systematic review and meta-analysis of direct comparative studies concluded tirzepatide was significantly superior to semaglutide in achieving weight reduction, with a pooled standardized mean difference of 0.75 (95% CI: 0.52 to 0.92).[5] A network meta-analysis in patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg showed statistically significant, greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight versus the GLP-1 receptor agonists dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[6]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:2648: 13: In randomized controlled trials, the overall safety profile of tirzepatide is similar to that of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs), with gastrointestinal adverse events being the most common.[7] A systematic review of RCTs found the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events with tirzepatide was consistent with GLP-1RAs.[8] The most frequently reported gastrointestinal events in a real-world pharmacovigilance analysis were nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea.[9] In a related population of patients with obesity or overweight, a network meta-analysis found tirzepatide showed a higher severe hypoglycemia risk (<54 mg/dL) compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[10] Real-world data also indicate hypoglycemia risk may be heightened by concomitant use of other hypoglycemic drugs.[9] A post-marketing surveillance analysis detected disproportionate reporting of gastrointestinal events, such as nausea (ROR 4.01, 95% CI 3.85–4.19), but concluded tirzepatide exhibited a similar risk of gastrointestinal adverse events compared to GLP-1RAs.[7]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:2652: 17: A network meta-analysis found that compared to the 5 mg dose, tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg provided significantly greater reductions in HbA1c (mean differences of -0.19% and -0.32%, respectively) and body weight (mean differences of -1.96 kg and -3.31 kg, respectively).[11] A separate analysis confirmed that tirzepatide 15 mg significantly reduced HbA1c by 0.30% and weight by 4.26 kg compared to the 5 mg dose.[12] This dose-response pattern for weight loss is also observed in a related obesity trial without diabetes, where higher tirzepatide doses (10 mg and 15 mg) were associated with higher proportions of participants achieving various weight reduction thresholds and greater mean percent weight reductions at 72 weeks compared to the 5 mg dose.[13] Regarding the time to achieve treatment goals, a pre-planned analysis of SURPASS trials reported that the median time to reach an HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all three tirzepatide doses, while the median time to first achieve ≥5% weight loss was 16.0 weeks for the 5 mg dose and 12.4 weeks for the 10 and 15 mg doses.[14] However, the increased efficacy of higher doses is accompanied by a higher risk of adverse events, particularly gastrointestinal events, with one analysis noting a significantly higher risk of any adverse event for the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[12] Consequently, one review concluded that while tirzepatide 15 mg provides the best efficacy for glycemic and weight reduction, the 5 mg dose offers a more favorable safety profile.[15]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:2656: 21: Tirzepatide is a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist.[16] A phase 1 clinical trial specifically assessed the effects of subcutaneous tirzepatide versus placebo or semaglutide on pancreatic islet function and insulin sensitivity in adults with type 2 diabetes.[17] A systematic review describes that the dual incretin agonism of tirzepatide enhances insulin secretion in a glucose-dependent manner and improves insulin sensitivity.[18] In a substudy of the SURPASS-3 trial, tirzepatide was shown to reduce liver fat content and abdominal adipose tissue in people with type 2 diabetes compared with insulin degludec.[19] The combined infusion of GIP and GLP-1 is noted to produce a more pronounced glucose-lowering effect and enhanced insulin secretion than the administration of either agent alone.[16] The reduction in liver fat and visceral adipose tissue is a mechanism by which tirzepatide may improve systemic insulin sensitivity.[19]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:2660: 25: Limitations: The corpus has significant tier-distribution gaps, with only 15% of sources being T1 primary studies and a heavy reliance on T4 (26%) and T7 (31%) material. The pipeline detected 12 contradictions, all concerning tirzepatide's effect on body weight or weight loss, where sources disagree substantially on the magnitude of the reported outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010 to the present, but the concentration of lower-tier sources and high-severity contradictions indicates potential reliability issues in the aggregated data.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:5572: M outputs/honest_sweep_r3/due_diligence/dd_lilly_tirzepatide_manufacturing/run_log.txt
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:5642:In the pivotal SURPASS phase 3 trials, all doses of tirzepatide demonstrated superiority in reducing A1C and body weight from baseline versus placebo or active comparators in adults with type 2 diabetes.[1] The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] In SURPASS-2, which compared tirzepatide to semaglutide 1 mg, reductions in A1C at week 40 were −2.09%, −2.37%, and −2.46% for tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg, respectively, versus −1.86% for semaglutide.[1] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the completed SURPASS trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] Body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine in SURPASS-5 to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor in SURPASS-3.[1] In SURPASS-6, adding tirzepatide to insulin glargine resulted in an estimated mean change from baseline in HbA1c of −2.1% versus −1.1% with added prandial insulin lispro at week 52.[2] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial in the Asia-Pacific region, all tirzepatide doses were superior to insulin glargine for HbA1c reduction, with reductions of −2.24%, −2.44%, and −2.49% for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus −0.95% for insulin glargine at week 40.[3] In the same trial, body weight reductions were −5.0 kg, −7.0 kg, and −7.2 kg for the respective tirzepatide doses compared to a 1.5 kg increase with insulin glargine.[3]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:5646:An indirect treatment comparison in people with obesity or overweight and type 2 diabetes estimated that tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg were associated with significantly greater mean percent weight reductions versus semaglutide 2.4 mg, with mean differences of 2.57% and 4.79%, respectively.[4] A separate systematic review and meta-analysis of direct comparative studies concluded tirzepatide was significantly superior to semaglutide in achieving weight reduction, with a pooled standardized mean difference of 0.75 (95% CI: 0.52 to 0.92).[5] A network meta-analysis in patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg showed statistically significant, greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight versus the GLP-1 receptor agonists dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[6]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:5650:In randomized controlled trials, the overall safety profile of tirzepatide is similar to that of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs), with gastrointestinal adverse events being the most common.[7] A systematic review of RCTs found the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events with tirzepatide was consistent with GLP-1RAs.[8] The most frequently reported gastrointestinal events in a real-world pharmacovigilance analysis were nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea.[9] In a related population of patients with obesity or overweight, a network meta-analysis found tirzepatide showed a higher severe hypoglycemia risk (<54 mg/dL) compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[10] Real-world data also indicate hypoglycemia risk may be heightened by concomitant use of other hypoglycemic drugs.[9] A post-marketing surveillance analysis detected disproportionate reporting of gastrointestinal events, such as nausea (ROR 4.01, 95% CI 3.85–4.19), but concluded tirzepatide exhibited a similar risk of gastrointestinal adverse events compared to GLP-1RAs.[7]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:5654:A network meta-analysis found that compared to the 5 mg dose, tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg provided significantly greater reductions in HbA1c (mean differences of -0.19% and -0.32%, respectively) and body weight (mean differences of -1.96 kg and -3.31 kg, respectively).[11] A separate analysis confirmed that tirzepatide 15 mg significantly reduced HbA1c by 0.30% and weight by 4.26 kg compared to the 5 mg dose.[12] This dose-response pattern for weight loss is also observed in a related obesity trial without diabetes, where higher tirzepatide doses (10 mg and 15 mg) were associated with higher proportions of participants achieving various weight reduction thresholds and greater mean percent weight reductions at 72 weeks compared to the 5 mg dose.[13] Regarding the time to achieve treatment goals, a pre-planned analysis of SURPASS trials reported that the median time to reach an HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all three tirzepatide doses, while the median time to first achieve ≥5% weight loss was 16.0 weeks for the 5 mg dose and 12.4 weeks for the 10 and 15 mg doses.[14] However, the increased efficacy of higher doses is accompanied by a higher risk of adverse events, particularly gastrointestinal events, with one analysis noting a significantly higher risk of any adverse event for the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[12] Consequently, one review concluded that while tirzepatide 15 mg provides the best efficacy for glycemic and weight reduction, the 5 mg dose offers a more favorable safety profile.[15]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:5658:Tirzepatide is a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist.[16] A phase 1 clinical trial specifically assessed the effects of subcutaneous tirzepatide versus placebo or semaglutide on pancreatic islet function and insulin sensitivity in adults with type 2 diabetes.[17] A systematic review describes that the dual incretin agonism of tirzepatide enhances insulin secretion in a glucose-dependent manner and improves insulin sensitivity.[18] In a substudy of the SURPASS-3 trial, tirzepatide was shown to reduce liver fat content and abdominal adipose tissue in people with type 2 diabetes compared with insulin degludec.[19] The combined infusion of GIP and GLP-1 is noted to produce a more pronounced glucose-lowering effect and enhanced insulin secretion than the administration of either agent alone.[16] The reduction in liver fat and visceral adipose tissue is a mechanism by which tirzepatide may improve systemic insulin sensitivity.[19]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:490:  "question": "What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:509:  "run_id": "SWEEP_clinical_clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm_1776623789",

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:516:# Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:520:Tirzepatide demonstrated significant, dose-dependent improvements in both glycemic control and body weight across the SURPASS clinical trials in adults with type 2 diabetes.[1] The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] In SURPASS-1, the highest dose (15 mg) reduced A1C by 2.07% from baseline and 52% of participants achieved normoglycemia (A1c <5.7%).[1][2] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] For weight loss, body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine (SURPASS-5) to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor (SURPASS-3).[1] Tirzepatide was superior to active comparators, including semaglutide 1 mg and various insulin regimens, in reducing A1C and body weight.[1][3] These effects were consistent across different patient populations and background therapies.[1][4]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:524:In a direct head-to-head trial in patients with obesity without diabetes, tirzepatide demonstrated superior weight loss compared to semaglutide, with a least-squares mean percent change in weight at week 72 of −20.2% versus −13.7%.[5] Participants were also more likely to achieve weight reductions of at least 10%, 15%, 20%, and 25% with tirzepatide.[5] Another phase 3 trial reported a mean percentage change in body weight with tirzepatide of −20.2% compared with −13.7% for semaglutide at 72 weeks.[6] A real-world retrospective cohort study in patients without diabetes also found greater 6-month mean percentage weight reduction with tirzepatide (−11.15%) versus semaglutide (−8.83%).[7] A systematic review and meta-analysis of direct comparative studies concluded tirzepatide was significantly superior to semaglutide in achieving weight reduction, with a greater standardized mean difference for weight loss.[8] For glycemic control in type 2 diabetes, a network meta-analysis found all tirzepatide doses (5, 10, 15 mg) were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c.[9] An indirect treatment comparison also concluded tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg were associated with significantly greater mean percent weight reductions versus semaglutide 2.4 mg in people with obesity and type 2 diabetes.[10] Compared to insulin, tirzepatide showed greater efficacy; a meta-analysis found once-weekly tirzepatide significantly decreased HbA1c and body weight compared with daily insulin glargine and degludec, with a lower risk of hypoglycemia.[11] Specifically, in a trial adding therapy to basal insulin, the mean change in HbA1c at week 52 was −2.1% with tirzepatide versus −1.1% with insulin lispro, with less hypoglycemia and more body weight reduction.[12] Another meta-analysis reported all three tirzepatide doses resulted in a significant reduction in HbA1c and a significant decrease in body weight compared with long-acting insulin.[13] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal events were the most common adverse events for both tirzepatide and semaglutide in the head-to-head obesity trial, with one source reporting at least one adverse event in approximately 76.7% of patients on tirzepatide and 79% on semaglutide.[5][6] A network meta-analysis in patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin found tirzepatide showed a statistically significant, higher likelihood of experiencing nausea compared with placebo or exenatide, but no significant difference in treatment discontinuation due to adverse events compared to several GLP-1 receptor agonists.[14]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:528:Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most commonly reported adverse events with tirzepatide and are dose-dependent, with reported incidences of 39% (95% CI, 35%-43%), 46% (95% CI, 42%-49%), and 49% (95% CI, 38%-60%) for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively.[15] Among gastrointestinal events, nausea and diarrhea are the most frequent at any dose.[15] Drug discontinuation due to adverse events was highest with the 15 mg dose at 10%.[15] Regarding hypoglycemia, a meta-analysis found patients treated with tirzepatide had no significantly increased risk compared with placebo overall (OR= 2.05, 95%CI:0.95 to 4.42, p=0.07).[16] Another analysis reported tirzepatide 15mg was associated with more hypoglycemia than GLP-1 receptor agonists (pooled RR=3.83, 95% CI [1.19- 12.30], P=0.02).[17] Conversely, a different study in patients achieving normoglycemia found low rates, with 98.5% not experiencing level 2 or 3 severe hypoglycemia (<54 mg/dL) and 91% not experiencing level 1 hypoglycemia (≤70 mg/dL).[18] Rates of fatal adverse events, severe hypoglycemia, acute pancreatitis, cholelithiasis, and cholecystitis are reported as extremely low (≤ 1%) across all doses.[15] An analysis of FDA adverse event reports suggested an association between tirzepatide and thyroid cancer (reporting odds ratio = 2.09, 95% CI: 1.51–2.89), though this was lower than associations for other GLP-1 receptor agonists.[19]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:532:The effects of tirzepatide on glycemic and weight outcomes are dose-dependent within the 5 mg to 15 mg range.[20] This analysis also noted that fasting serum glucose showed improvement with the 15 mg dose.[21] One review concluded that within the 5–15 mg dose range, the effect in lowering glycemia and reducing weight is dose-dependent, with the 15 mg dose achieving the best efficacy benefit.[20] A separate meta-analysis confirmed that the 15 mg dose provided the greatest reductions in HbA1c and body weight compared to the 10 mg and 5 mg doses.[22] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal adverse events increased with higher doses, though one analysis reported this increase was without statistical significance.[21] Another analysis found the risk of total adverse events was significantly higher with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[22]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:536:In a post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-1 to -5, tirzepatide demonstrated significantly greater reductions in HbA1c versus comparators in both prespecified age subgroups (<65 and ≥65 years).[23] Regarding sex, all tirzepatide doses significantly reduced body weight compared to comparators regardless of the sex of study participants across SURPASS-1 to -5.[24] In patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and obesity, tirzepatide produced less weight loss at 52 weeks in those with type 2 diabetes (10.4%) than in those without diabetes (12.9%).[25] However, the effect of tirzepatide on the primary composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure was similar between patients with or without diabetes, with a hazard ratio of 0.64 for patients with diabetes and 0.61 for those without.[25] In a retrospective study of adults with type 1 diabetes and obesity, tirzepatide led to significant weight loss, with a median total body weight loss of 8% at 6 months and a 1% reduction in HbA1c.[26] For individuals with type 2 diabetes who were glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibody-positive, tirzepatide was effective, achieving HbA1c reductions of -2.11% and body weight reductions of -9.2 kg at 40/42 weeks, though HbA1c reductions were slightly greater in GADA-negative patients.[27] The most frequent adverse events across subgroups were mild-to-moderate and gastrointestinal-related, occurring during dose-escalation.[23][24]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:540:Limitations: The corpus exhibits significant tier-distribution gaps, with only 15% of sources being T1 primary studies and a heavy reliance on T7 sources at 32%. The pipeline detected 11 high-severity contradictions, primarily concerning tirzepatide's effect on body weight, where sources disagree substantially on the magnitude of the reported outcome. The evidence horizon spans from 2010 to the present, which may not capture earlier foundational research.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:2554:    "statement": "Optimal dose of tirzepatide for type 2 diabetes mellitus: A meta-analysis and trial sequential analysis",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:2617:# Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:2621:In SURPASS-3, the highest dose (15 mg) reduced HbA1c by 2.37% and body weight by 12.9 kg (13.9%).[1] Similarly, in SURPASS-5, the 15 mg dose reduced HbA1c by 2.59% and body weight by 10.9 kg (11.6%).[1] A systematic review and meta-analysis found tirzepatide treatment resulted in a greater lowering of HbA1c (–1.94%, 95% CI: –2.02 to –1.87) and body weight (–8.47 kg, 95% CI: –9.66 to –7.27) compared to control.[2] Another meta-analysis reported tirzepatide reduced HbA1c (WMD: -1.07%; 95% CI: −1.44, −0.56) and body weight (WMD: −7.99 kg; 95% CI −11.36, −4.62).[3] The proportion of participants achieving an HbA1c value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the SURPASS trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[4] One source reports that between 13% and 27% of patients receiving tirzepatide in SURPASS-1 achieved weight loss of at least 15% of body weight.[5] In SURPASS-2, a greater proportion of tirzepatide patients achieved HbA1c goals at 40 weeks compared to semaglutide and a significantly greater proportion achieved their weight loss goals.[5]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:2625:In a head-to-head trial in patients with type 2 diabetes, all doses of tirzepatide (5, 10, and 15 mg) were superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg for reductions in HbA1c, fasting serum glucose, and body weight at 40 weeks.[6] This finding of superior weight loss for tirzepatide is supported by a systematic review and meta-analysis of direct comparative studies, which reported a greater standardized mean difference for weight reduction favoring tirzepatide.[7] An indirect treatment comparison in people with obesity and type 2 diabetes also found tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg were associated with significantly greater mean percent weight reductions versus semaglutide 2.4 mg, with mean differences of 2.57% and 4.79%, respectively.[8] Another systematic review concluded all three tirzepatide doses were more effective than long-acting insulin in reducing HbA1c and achieving weight loss targets, with the 15 mg dose showing the largest effects.[9] The safety profile for both tirzepatide and semaglutide is characterized predominantly by gastrointestinal adverse events, with one source noting the most common adverse events in a head-to-head obesity trial were gastrointestinal and mostly mild to moderate in severity for both treatments.[10]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:2629:The overall safety profile of tirzepatide is similar to GLP-1 receptor agonists, primarily characterized by gastrointestinal adverse events.[11] A systematic review found the incidences of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea were higher than placebo but consistent with GLP-1 RAs.[11] Treatment-emergent anti-drug antibodies developed in 51.1% of patients across Phase 3 trials, but these had no discernible impact on pharmacokinetics or efficacy.[12] A real-world pharmacovigilance analysis detected disproportionate reporting of medullary thyroid cancer with tirzepatide (ROR 13.67, 95% CI 4.35–42.96).[13] This analysis concluded tirzepatide exhibited a similar risk of medullary thyroid cancer compared to GLP-1RAs, and it carries a black box warning regarding the unknown risk of thyroid C-cell tumors in humans.[13][14] A case report also describes a patient developing painless biphasic thyroiditis following tirzepatide use.[15]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:2633:Tirzepatide's effects on glycemic control and weight loss are generally dose-dependent across the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses.[16] One network meta-analysis reported significant reductions in HbA1c with tirzepatide 10 mg (mean difference: -0.19%) and 15 mg (mean difference: -0.32%) compared to the 5 mg dose.[17] Similarly, for weight reduction, the same analysis found notable effects for the 10 mg (mean difference: -1.96 kg) and 15 mg (mean difference: -3.31 kg) doses versus 5 mg.[17] Another meta-analysis found that compared to the 5 mg dose, the 10 mg dose significantly reduced HbA1c by 0.20% and weight by 2.64 kg, while the 15 mg dose reduced HbA1c by 0.30% and weight by 4.26 kg.[18] This analysis also indicated that for reducing HbA1c and weight, tirzepatide 15 mg performed the best, followed by 10 mg and then 5 mg.[18] A separate meta-analysis concluded that within the 5–15 mg range, the effect of tirzepatide in lowering glycemia and reducing weight is dose-dependent, with the 15 mg dose achieving the best efficacy benefit.[19] The weight reductions were generally greater in higher baseline BMI categories, though the effect was present irrespective of BMI.[16] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal events increased with higher doses, though one analysis reported this was without statistical significance, while another found the risk of total adverse events was significantly higher with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[17][18]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:2637:A post-hoc subgroup analysis of the SURPASS trials found that all tirzepatide doses significantly reduced body weight compared to placebo or active comparators regardless of the sex of study participants (p<0.001).[20] In prespecified age subgroups, analyses of change from baseline in HbA1c at 40 or 52 weeks for subjects <65 years and ≥65 years were consistent with the primary study results, with treatment differences favoring all three doses of tirzepatide compared with placebo or active comparator.[21] In a post hoc analysis of SURPASS 2-5, tirzepatide was effective in reducing HbA1c and body weight irrespective of glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibody (GADA) status in adults with type 2 diabetes.[22] At 40/42 weeks, GADA-positive patients achieved reductions in HbA1c of -2.11% and body weight of -9.2 kg, while GADA-negative patients achieved reductions of -2.32% and -9.6 kg, respectively (p<0.001 for both groups).[22] Reductions in HbA1c were slightly greater in GADA-negative patients (estimated treatment difference 0.21% [0.03, 0.39]; p=0.024), whereas reductions in body weight did not significantly differ between groups (0.38 kg [-0.99, 1.75]; p=0.588).[22] In a prespecified analysis of patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and obesity, tirzepatide produced less weight loss at 52 weeks in patients with type 2 diabetes (10.4% [95% CI: 8.7%-12.2%]) than in patients without diabetes (12.9% [95% CI: 11.2%-14.6%]) (Pinteraction = 0.04).[23] However, the effect of tirzepatide on the primary composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure event was similar for patients with or without diabetes, with a hazard ratio of 0.64 (95% CI: 0.35-1.15) in patients with diabetes and 0.61 (95% CI: 0.33-1.10) in patients without diabetes (Pinteraction = 0.95).[23] A retrospective study in adults with type 1 diabetes and obesity reported a median total body weight loss of 8% [5-14] and a decrease in HbA1c of 1% [0.2-1.8] from baseline to last follow-up (median 6 months), with no episodes of severe hypoglycemia or diabetic ketoacidosis recorded.[24]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:2641:Limitations: The corpus is heavily skewed toward lower-tier sources, with only 7% of sources being T1 primary studies. The pipeline detected 13 high-severity contradictions, primarily concerning tirzepatide's effect on body weight and weight loss, where sources disagree dramatically on the magnitude of the reported outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010 to the present, but the low proportion of high-tier evidence limits the strength of conclusions that can be drawn.
