.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:3091:"C:\\Program Files\\PowerShell\\7\\pwsh.exe" -Command '$i=0; Get-Content outputs/full_scale_v5/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md | ForEach-Object { $i++; '"'{0,4}: {1}' -f "'$i, $_ }' in C:\POLARIS
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:3093:   1: # Research report: What is the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for glycemic control and weight loss in adults with type 2 diabetes?
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:3097:   5: Tirzepatide demonstrated significant, dose-dependent improvements in both glycemic control and body weight across the SURPASS clinical trials in adults with type 2 diabetes.[1] The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] In SURPASS-1, the highest dose (15 mg) reduced A1C by 2.07% from baseline and 52% of participants achieved normoglycemia (A1c <5.7%).[1][2] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] For weight loss, body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine (SURPASS-5) to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor (SURPASS-3).[1] Tirzepatide was superior to active comparators, including semaglutide 1 mg and various insulin regimens, in reducing A1C and body weight.[1][3] These effects were consistent across different patient populations and background therapies.[1][4]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:3101:   9: In a direct head-to-head trial in patients with obesity without diabetes, tirzepatide demonstrated superior weight loss compared to semaglutide, with a least-squares mean percent change in weight at week 72 of −20.2% versus −13.7%.[5] Participants were also more likely to achieve weight reductions of at least 10%, 15%, 20%, and 25% with tirzepatide.[5] Another phase 3 trial reported a mean percentage change in body weight with tirzepatide of −20.2% compared with −13.7% for semaglutide at 72 weeks.[6] A real-world retrospective cohort study in patients without diabetes also found greater 6-month mean percentage weight reduction with tirzepatide (−11.15%) versus semaglutide (−8.83%).[7] A systematic review and meta-analysis of direct comparative studies concluded tirzepatide was significantly superior to semaglutide in achieving weight reduction, with a greater standardized mean difference for weight loss.[8] For glycemic control in type 2 diabetes, a network meta-analysis found all tirzepatide doses (5, 10, 15 mg) were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c.[9] An indirect treatment comparison also concluded tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg were associated with significantly greater mean percent weight reductions versus semaglutide 2.4 mg in people with obesity and type 2 diabetes.[10] Compared to insulin, tirzepatide showed greater efficacy; a meta-analysis found once-weekly tirzepatide significantly decreased HbA1c and body weight compared with daily insulin glargine and degludec, with a lower risk of hypoglycemia.[11] Specifically, in a trial adding therapy to basal insulin, the mean change in HbA1c at week 52 was −2.1% with tirzepatide versus −1.1% with insulin lispro, with less hypoglycemia and more body weight reduction.[12] Another meta-analysis reported all three tirzepatide doses resulted in a significant reduction in HbA1c and a significant decrease in body weight compared with long-acting insulin.[13] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal events were the most common adverse events for both tirzepatide and semaglutide in the head-to-head obesity trial, with one source reporting at least one adverse event in approximately 76.7% of patients on tirzepatide and 79% on semaglutide.[5][6] A network meta-analysis in patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin found tirzepatide showed a statistically significant, higher likelihood of experiencing nausea compared with placebo or exenatide, but no significant difference in treatment discontinuation due to adverse events compared to several GLP-1 receptor agonists.[14]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:3105:  13: Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most commonly reported adverse events with tirzepatide and are dose-dependent, with reported incidences of 39% (95% CI, 35%-43%), 46% (95% CI, 42%-49%), and 49% (95% CI, 38%-60%) for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively.[15] Among gastrointestinal events, nausea and diarrhea are the most frequent at any dose.[15] Drug discontinuation due to adverse events was highest with the 15 mg dose at 10%.[15] Regarding hypoglycemia, a meta-analysis found patients treated with tirzepatide had no significantly increased risk compared with placebo overall (OR= 2.05, 95%CI:0.95 to 4.42, p=0.07).[16] Another analysis reported tirzepatide 15mg was associated with more hypoglycemia than GLP-1 receptor agonists (pooled RR=3.83, 95% CI [1.19- 12.30], P=0.02).[17] Conversely, a different study in patients achieving normoglycemia found low rates, with 98.5% not experiencing level 2 or 3 severe hypoglycemia (<54 mg/dL) and 91% not experiencing level 1 hypoglycemia (≤70 mg/dL).[18] Rates of fatal adverse events, severe hypoglycemia, acute pancreatitis, cholelithiasis, and cholecystitis are reported as extremely low (≤ 1%) across all doses.[15] An analysis of FDA adverse event reports suggested an association between tirzepatide and thyroid cancer (reporting odds ratio = 2.09, 95% CI: 1.51–2.89), though this was lower than associations for other GLP-1 receptor agonists.[19]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:3109:  17: The effects of tirzepatide on glycemic and weight outcomes are dose-dependent within the 5 mg to 15 mg range.[20] This analysis also noted that fasting serum glucose showed improvement with the 15 mg dose.[21] One review concluded that within the 5–15 mg dose range, the effect in lowering glycemia and reducing weight is dose-dependent, with the 15 mg dose achieving the best efficacy benefit.[20] A separate meta-analysis confirmed that the 15 mg dose provided the greatest reductions in HbA1c and body weight compared to the 10 mg and 5 mg doses.[22] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal adverse events increased with higher doses, though one analysis reported this increase was without statistical significance.[21] Another analysis found the risk of total adverse events was significantly higher with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[22]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:3113:  21: In a post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-1 to -5, tirzepatide demonstrated significantly greater reductions in HbA1c versus comparators in both prespecified age subgroups (<65 and ≥65 years).[23] Regarding sex, all tirzepatide doses significantly reduced body weight compared to comparators regardless of the sex of study participants across SURPASS-1 to -5.[24] In patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and obesity, tirzepatide produced less weight loss at 52 weeks in those with type 2 diabetes (10.4%) than in those without diabetes (12.9%).[25] However, the effect of tirzepatide on the primary composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure was similar between patients with or without diabetes, with a hazard ratio of 0.64 for patients with diabetes and 0.61 for those without.[25] In a retrospective study of adults with type 1 diabetes and obesity, tirzepatide led to significant weight loss, with a median total body weight loss of 8% at 6 months and a 1% reduction in HbA1c.[26] For individuals with type 2 diabetes who were glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibody-positive, tirzepatide was effective, achieving HbA1c reductions of -2.11% and body weight reductions of -9.2 kg at 40/42 weeks, though HbA1c reductions were slightly greater in GADA-negative patients.[27] The most frequent adverse events across subgroups were mild-to-moderate and gastrointestinal-related, occurring during dose-escalation.[23][24]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_2\codex_stderr.log:3117:  25: Limitations: The corpus exhibits significant tier-distribution gaps, with only 15% of sources being T1 primary studies and a heavy reliance on T7 sources at 32%. The pipeline detected 11 high-severity contradictions, primarily concerning tirzepatide's effect on body weight, where sources disagree substantially on the magnitude of the reported outcome. The evidence horizon spans from 2010 to the present, which may not capture earlier foundational research.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1093:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:1022:  765: 19.​Lilly's SURMOUNT-2 results published in The Lancet show tirzepatide achieved a 
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1097:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:1042:  785: with tirzepatide: a post hoc analysis from the SURPASS Clinical Trial Program - 
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1105:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:1126:    9: Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most commonly reported safety concern with tirzepatide and exhibit a dose-dependent relationship.[4] A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized trials in type 2 diabetes found tirzepatide increased the risk of gastrointestinal adverse events compared to control, primarily in add-on therapy.[5][6] Pooled estimates from ten trials (N=6836) reported gastrointestinal adverse event rates of 39% (95% CI, 35%-43%), 46% (95% CI, 42%-49%), and 49% (95% CI, 38%-60%) for the 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses, respectively.[4] Nausea and diarrhea are the most frequent specific events at any dose.[4] The intrinsic risk of hypoglycemia with tirzepatide monotherapy or when combined with agents like metformin is low due to its glucose-dependent mechanism.[7] However, the risk increases substantially when tirzepatide is combined with insulin or sulfonylureas.[5][7] One network meta-analysis found tirzepatide showed a higher severe hypoglycemia risk compared to semaglutide and liraglutide in patients with obesity or overweight.[8] A disproportionality analysis of FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS) data found a significant reporting odds ratio (ROR) of 13.67 (95% CI 4.35–42.96) for medullary thyroid carcinoma with tirzepatide.[9] Another FAERS analysis identified 3 cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma with a significant signal strength (EB05=3.55).[10] Case reports also describe thyroid dysfunction, such as a single case of painless biphasic thyroiditis following tirzepatide initiation that resolved after discontinuation.[11] The overall incidence of serious adverse events, acute pancreatitis, and cholelithiasis/cholecystitis across trials is extremely low (≤1%).[4]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1106:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:1130:   13: This trial also reported significantly greater weight loss with all tirzepatide doses, with mean reductions of -7.6 kg, -9.3 kg, and -11.2 kg versus -5.7 kg with semaglutide.[12][2] A post hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found that a higher proportion of participants achieved stringent composite therapeutic targets (e.g., HbA1c <7.0% with ≥10% weight loss) with tirzepatide compared to semaglutide.[13] A comprehensive review noted the safety profiles of both agents are consistent with the incretin class, characterized predominantly by transient gastrointestinal events.[14] A network meta-analysis in patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin background therapy found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg provided statistically significant, greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight at the primary endpoint versus the GLP-1 receptor agonists dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[15]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1107:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:1134:   17: In a phase 1 clinical trial specifically designed to assess islet function and insulin sensitivity, subcutaneous tirzepatide demonstrated effects on pancreatic islet function versus placebo or semaglutide in adults with type 2 diabetes.[16] GIP's role is nuanced; it stimulates glucose-dependent insulin secretion and, uniquely, also stimulates glucagon secretion during hyperglycemia, which one review suggests may subsequently enhance insulin secretion through intra-islet paracrine communication.[17] This contrasts with the historical view that GIP's glucagon stimulation was uniformly detrimental in type 2 diabetes.[17] In phase 2 trials, tirzepatide demonstrated dose-dependent improvements in beta-cell function, as measured by the Homeostatic Model Assessment 2 of beta-cell function (HOMA2-B) index.[18] The combined incretin action results in robust glycemic control, with one review of the SURPASS trials reporting that a greater proportion of participants receiving tirzepatide achieved HbA1c levels below 5.7% across baseline HbA1c categories compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[19]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1108:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:1138:   21: A systematic review and meta-analysis of phase II/III trials confirmed that tirzepatide treatment resulted in a pooled mean HbA1c reduction of −1.94% and body weight reduction of −8.47 kg, with greater efficacy at higher doses.[20] Another meta-analysis reported similar incremental benefits: HbA1c was reduced by −0.20% with 10 mg and −0.30% with 15 mg versus 5 mg, and body weight was reduced by −2.64 kg with 10 mg and −4.26 kg with 15 mg versus 5 mg.[21] A Bayesian network meta-analysis ranked the 15 mg dose as having the highest probability (93.5%) of being the best option for HbA1c reduction and the highest probability (99.7%) for body weight reduction among active comparators.[3] The time to achieve glycemic targets also demonstrates a dose-response relationship; a pre-planned analysis of SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 found the median time to achieve HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all three tirzepatide doses, faster than with semaglutide 1 mg (12.0 weeks) or insulin degludec (12.1 weeks).[22] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal adverse events are the most common and show a dose-dependent increase in incidence.[23] Another analysis found the risk of adverse events was higher for the 10 mg (RR 1.20) and 15 mg (RR 1.26) doses compared to 5 mg.[21] Trial data corroborate this pattern; in SURPASS J-mono, the incidence of nausea was 12%, 20%, and 20% for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus 8% with dulaglutide 0.75 mg.[1] However, the incidence of serious adverse events and treatment discontinuation due to adverse events does not consistently show a clear dose-response trend across all trials.[23][21] A subgroup analysis of SURPASS-AP-Combo found the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events was generally similar across patient subgroups defined by baseline characteristics like age, BMI, and HbA1c for each tirzepatide dose.[24][25]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1110:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:1142:   25: Tirzepatide is approved under two distinct proprietary names in the United States: Mounjaro for type 2 diabetes mellitus and Zepbound for chronic weight management.[26][27] The US FDA approved Mounjaro (tirzepatide) injection on May 15, 2022, as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[26] Subsequently, the FDA approved Zepbound (tirzepatide) injection on November 8, 2023, as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for chronic weight management in adults with an initial body mass index (BMI) of 30 kg/m² or greater (obesity) or 27 kg/m² or greater (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbid condition.[27] In December 2024, the FDA expanded the Zepbound label to include treatment of moderate to severe obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) in adults with obesity.[28] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) granted a marketing authorization valid throughout the EU for Mounjaro on September 15, 2022, for the treatment of adults with insufficiently controlled type 2 diabetes as an adjunct to diet and exercise, either as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or as an add-on to other diabetes medicines.[29][30] The EMA also approved Mounjaro for weight management as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for weight loss and weight maintenance in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbidity.[31] The US prescribing information for both products carries a boxed warning for risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, stating that tirzepatide causes thyroid C-cell tumors in rats and that it is unknown whether it causes such tumors, including medullary thyroid carcinoma, in humans.[32][33] Consequently, both Mounjaro and Zepbound are contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or in patients with Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2).[32][33] The labels contain warnings and precautions for severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, acute kidney injury due to volume depletion, acute gallbladder disease, acute pancreatitis, hypersensitivity reactions, hypoglycemia with concomitant use of insulin secretagogues or insulin, diabetic retinopathy complications in patients with type 2 diabetes, and pulmonary aspiration during general anesthesia or deep sedation.[32][33] The Zepbound label also includes a specific warning to never share a Zepbound KwikPen between patients.[32] The Canadian Product Monograph similarly includes a Serious Warnings and Precautions box for thyroid C-cell tumors and contraindicates use in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN 2.[34] The recommended dosing regimen is consistent across indications and jurisdictions: therapy is initiated at 2.5 mg injected subcutaneously once weekly, with the dose increased in 2.5 mg increments after at least 4 weeks on the current dose based on treatment response and tolerability.[35][36][32][33] For glycemic control in type 2 diabetes, the maximum recommended dosage for adults is 15 mg once weekly, while for pediatric patients 10 years and older, the maximum is 10 mg once weekly.[33] For chronic weight management and obstructive sleep apnea, the recommended maintenance dosages are 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once weekly, with a maximum of 15 mg.[32] The drug product is supplied as a solution for injection in single-dose pens or vials across multiple strengths (2.5 mg/0.5 mL, 5 mg/0.5 mL, 7.5 mg/0.5 mL, 10 mg/0.5 mL, 12.5 mg/0.5 mL, 15 mg/0.5 mL).[32][33] The pharmacokinetics are well characterized, with an absolute bioavailability of approximately 80%, a time to maximum concentration ranging from 8 to 72 hours, and a terminal half-life of approximately 5 days supporting once-weekly administration.[35][36] In the UK, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommends tirzepatide (Mounjaro) for treating type 2 diabetes alongside diet and exercise in adults only under specific criteria: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral antidiabetic drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of at least 35 kg/m² with associated psychological or medical problems, or a BMI of less than 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy would have significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other significant obesity-related complications.[37][38] Lower BMI thresholds (usually reduced by 2.5 kg/m²) apply for people from South Asian, Chinese, other Asian, Middle Eastern, Black African, or African-Caribbean family backgrounds.[37] For weight management, NICE recommends tirzepatide (Zepbound) as an option for adults with an initial BMI of at least 35 kg/m² and at least one weight-related comorbidity, with the same ethnic BMI adjustments, and recommends assessing treatment response after 6 months on the highest tolerated dose.[39][40]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1293:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2899:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json:194:    "statement": "FDA Approves First Medication for Obstructive Sleep Apnea",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1294:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2900:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json:201:    "statement": "Mounjaro | European Medicines Agency (EMA)",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1295:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2901:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json:208:    "statement": "[PDF] mounjaro-epar-public-assessment-report_en.pdf - EMA",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1296:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2902:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json:215:    "statement": "[PDF] Mounjaro, INN-tirzepatide - EMA",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1297:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2903:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json:257:    "statement": "[PDF] Tirzepatide for treating type 2 diabetes | NICE",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1298:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2904:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json:271:    "statement": "Overweight and obesity management | Guidance - NICE",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1299:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2905:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json:278:    "statement": "[PDF] Tirzepatide: local formulary information | NICE",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1300:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2906:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:9:Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most commonly reported safety concern with tirzepatide and exhibit a dose-dependent relationship.[4] A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized trials in type 2 diabetes found tirzepatide increased the risk of gastrointestinal adverse events compared to control, primarily in add-on therapy.[5][6] Pooled estimates from ten trials (N=6836) reported gastrointestinal adverse event rates of 39% (95% CI, 35%-43%), 46% (95% CI, 42%-49%), and 49% (95% CI, 38%-60%) for the 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses, respectively.[4] Nausea and diarrhea are the most frequent specific events at any dose.[4] The intrinsic risk of hypoglycemia with tirzepatide monotherapy or when combined with agents like metformin is low due to its glucose-dependent mechanism.[7] However, the risk increases substantially when tirzepatide is combined with insulin or sulfonylureas.[5][7] One network meta-analysis found tirzepatide showed a higher severe hypoglycemia risk compared to semaglutide and liraglutide in patients with obesity or overweight.[8] A disproportionality analysis of FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS) data found a significant reporting odds ratio (ROR) of 13.67 (95% CI 4.35–42.96) for medullary thyroid carcinoma with tirzepatide.[9] Another FAERS analysis identified 3 cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma with a significant signal strength (EB05=3.55).[10] Case reports also describe thyroid dysfunction, such as a single case of painless biphasic thyroiditis following tirzepatide initiation that resolved after discontinuation.[11] The overall incidence of serious adverse events, acute pancreatitis, and cholelithiasis/cholecystitis across trials is extremely low (≤1%).[4]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1301:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2907:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:13:This trial also reported significantly greater weight loss with all tirzepatide doses, with mean reductions of -7.6 kg, -9.3 kg, and -11.2 kg versus -5.7 kg with semaglutide.[12][2] A post hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found that a higher proportion of participants achieved stringent composite therapeutic targets (e.g., HbA1c <7.0% with ≥10% weight loss) with tirzepatide compared to semaglutide.[13] A comprehensive review noted the safety profiles of both agents are consistent with the incretin class, characterized predominantly by transient gastrointestinal events.[14] A network meta-analysis in patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin background therapy found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg provided statistically significant, greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight at the primary endpoint versus the GLP-1 receptor agonists dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[15]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1302:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2909:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:17:In a phase 1 clinical trial specifically designed to assess islet function and insulin sensitivity, subcutaneous tirzepatide demonstrated effects on pancreatic islet function versus placebo or semaglutide in adults with type 2 diabetes.[16] GIP's role is nuanced; it stimulates glucose-dependent insulin secretion and, uniquely, also stimulates glucagon secretion during hyperglycemia, which one review suggests may subsequently enhance insulin secretion through intra-islet paracrine communication.[17] This contrasts with the historical view that GIP's glucagon stimulation was uniformly detrimental in type 2 diabetes.[17] In phase 2 trials, tirzepatide demonstrated dose-dependent improvements in beta-cell function, as measured by the Homeostatic Model Assessment 2 of beta-cell function (HOMA2-B) index.[18] The combined incretin action results in robust glycemic control, with one review of the SURPASS trials reporting that a greater proportion of participants receiving tirzepatide achieved HbA1c levels below 5.7% across baseline HbA1c categories compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[19]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1303:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2910:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:21:A systematic review and meta-analysis of phase II/III trials confirmed that tirzepatide treatment resulted in a pooled mean HbA1c reduction of −1.94% and body weight reduction of −8.47 kg, with greater efficacy at higher doses.[20] Another meta-analysis reported similar incremental benefits: HbA1c was reduced by −0.20% with 10 mg and −0.30% with 15 mg versus 5 mg, and body weight was reduced by −2.64 kg with 10 mg and −4.26 kg with 15 mg versus 5 mg.[21] A Bayesian network meta-analysis ranked the 15 mg dose as having the highest probability (93.5%) of being the best option for HbA1c reduction and the highest probability (99.7%) for body weight reduction among active comparators.[3] The time to achieve glycemic targets also demonstrates a dose-response relationship; a pre-planned analysis of SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 found the median time to achieve HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all three tirzepatide doses, faster than with semaglutide 1 mg (12.0 weeks) or insulin degludec (12.1 weeks).[22] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal adverse events are the most common and show a dose-dependent increase in incidence.[23] Another analysis found the risk of adverse events was higher for the 10 mg (RR 1.20) and 15 mg (RR 1.26) doses compared to 5 mg.[21] Trial data corroborate this pattern; in SURPASS J-mono, the incidence of nausea was 12%, 20%, and 20% for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus 8% with dulaglutide 0.75 mg.[1] However, the incidence of serious adverse events and treatment discontinuation due to adverse events does not consistently show a clear dose-response trend across all trials.[23][21] A subgroup analysis of SURPASS-AP-Combo found the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events was generally similar across patient subgroups defined by baseline characteristics like age, BMI, and HbA1c for each tirzepatide dose.[24][25]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1304:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2911:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:25:Tirzepatide is approved under two distinct proprietary names in the United States: Mounjaro for type 2 diabetes mellitus and Zepbound for chronic weight management.[26][27] The US FDA approved Mounjaro (tirzepatide) injection on May 15, 2022, as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[26] Subsequently, the FDA approved Zepbound (tirzepatide) injection on November 8, 2023, as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for chronic weight management in adults with an initial body mass index (BMI) of 30 kg/m² or greater (obesity) or 27 kg/m² or greater (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbid condition.[27] In December 2024, the FDA expanded the Zepbound label to include treatment of moderate to severe obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) in adults with obesity.[28] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) granted a marketing authorization valid throughout the EU for Mounjaro on September 15, 2022, for the treatment of adults with insufficiently controlled type 2 diabetes as an adjunct to diet and exercise, either as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or as an add-on to other diabetes medicines.[29][30] The EMA also approved Mounjaro for weight management as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for weight loss and weight maintenance in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbidity.[31] The US prescribing information for both products carries a boxed warning for risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, stating that tirzepatide causes thyroid C-cell tumors in rats and that it is unknown whether it causes such tumors, including medullary thyroid carcinoma, in humans.[32][33] Consequently, both Mounjaro and Zepbound are contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or in patients with Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2).[32][33] The labels contain warnings and precautions for severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, acute kidney injury due to volume depletion, acute gallbladder disease, acute pancreatitis, hypersensitivity reactions, hypoglycemia with concomitant use of insulin secretagogues or insulin, diabetic retinopathy complications in patients with type 2 diabetes, and pulmonary aspiration during general anesthesia or deep sedation.[32][33] The Zepbound label also includes a specific warning to never share a Zepbound KwikPen between patients.[32] The Canadian Product Monograph similarly includes a Serious Warnings and Precautions box for thyroid C-cell tumors and contraindicates use in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN 2.[34] The recommended dosing regimen is consistent across indications and jurisdictions: therapy is initiated at 2.5 mg injected subcutaneously once weekly, with the dose increased in 2.5 mg increments after at least 4 weeks on the current dose based on treatment response and tolerability.[35][36][32][33] For glycemic control in type 2 diabetes, the maximum recommended dosage for adults is 15 mg once weekly, while for pediatric patients 10 years and older, the maximum is 10 mg once weekly.[33] For chronic weight management and obstructive sleep apnea, the recommended maintenance dosages are 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once weekly, with a maximum of 15 mg.[32] The drug product is supplied as a solution for injection in single-dose pens or vials across multiple strengths (2.5 mg/0.5 mL, 5 mg/0.5 mL, 7.5 mg/0.5 mL, 10 mg/0.5 mL, 12.5 mg/0.5 mL, 15 mg/0.5 mL).[32][33] The pharmacokinetics are well characterized, with an absolute bioavailability of approximately 80%, a time to maximum concentration ranging from 8 to 72 hours, and a terminal half-life of approximately 5 days supporting once-weekly administration.[35][36] In the UK, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommends tirzepatide (Mounjaro) for treating type 2 diabetes alongside diet and exercise in adults only under specific criteria: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral antidiabetic drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of at least 35 kg/m² with associated psychological or medical problems, or a BMI of less than 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy would have significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other significant obesity-related complications.[37][38] Lower BMI thresholds (usually reduced by 2.5 kg/m²) apply for people from South Asian, Chinese, other Asian, Middle Eastern, Black African, or African-Caribbean family backgrounds.[37] For weight management, NICE recommends tirzepatide (Zepbound) as an option for adults with an initial BMI of at least 35 kg/m² and at least one weight-related comorbidity, with the same ethnic BMI adjustments, and recommends assessing treatment response after 6 months on the highest tolerated dose.[39][40]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1308:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2920:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:89:[24] Efficacy and Safety of Tirzepatide in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes: Analysis of SURPASS-AP-Combo by Different Subgroups — https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/s13300-024-01561-2.pdf (tier T1)
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1309:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2921:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:93:[28] FDA Approves First Medication for Obstructive Sleep Apnea — https://www.fda.gov/news-events/press-announcements/fda-approves-first-medication-obstructive-sleep-apnea (tier T3)
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1310:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2922:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:94:[29] Mounjaro | European Medicines Agency (EMA) — https://www.ema.europa.eu/en/medicines/human/EPAR/mounjaro (tier T3)
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1311:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2923:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:95:[30] [PDF] mounjaro-epar-public-assessment-report_en.pdf - EMA — https://www.ema.europa.eu/en/documents/assessment-report/mounjaro-epar-public-assessment-report_en.pdf (tier T3)
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1312:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2924:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:96:[31] [PDF] Mounjaro, INN-tirzepatide - EMA — https://www.ema.europa.eu/en/documents/variation-report/mounjaro-h-c-005620-ii-0027-epar-assessment-report-variation_en.pdf (tier T3)
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1313:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2925:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:102:[37] [PDF] Tirzepatide for treating type 2 diabetes | NICE — https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta924/resources/tirzepatide-for-treating-type-2-diabetes-pdf-82615547603653 (tier T3)
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1314:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2926:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:104:[39] Overweight and obesity management | Guidance - NICE — https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng246/resources/a-guide-for-prescribing-medicines-to-manage-overweight-and-obesity-15299628589/chapter/Prescribing-reviewing-and-stopping-tirzepatide (tier T3)
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1315:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2927:outputs/full_scale_v25/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:105:[40] [PDF] Tirzepatide: local formulary information | NICE — https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta1026/resources/tirzepatide-local-formulary-information-pdf-17647359110341 (tier T3)
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1316:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2942:    9: Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most commonly reported safety concern with tirzepatide and exhibit a dose-dependent relationship.[4] A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized trials in type 2 diabetes found tirzepatide increased the risk of gastrointestinal adverse events compared to control, primarily in add-on therapy.[5][6] Pooled estimates from ten trials (N=6836) reported gastrointestinal adverse event rates of 39% (95% CI, 35%-43%), 46% (95% CI, 42%-49%), and 49% (95% CI, 38%-60%) for the 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses, respectively.[4] Nausea and diarrhea are the most frequent specific events at any dose.[4] The intrinsic risk of hypoglycemia with tirzepatide monotherapy or when combined with agents like metformin is low due to its glucose-dependent mechanism.[7] However, the risk increases substantially when tirzepatide is combined with insulin or sulfonylureas.[5][7] One network meta-analysis found tirzepatide showed a higher severe hypoglycemia risk compared to semaglutide and liraglutide in patients with obesity or overweight.[8] A disproportionality analysis of FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS) data found a significant reporting odds ratio (ROR) of 13.67 (95% CI 4.35–42.96) for medullary thyroid carcinoma with tirzepatide.[9] Another FAERS analysis identified 3 cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma with a significant signal strength (EB05=3.55).[10] Case reports also describe thyroid dysfunction, such as a single case of painless biphasic thyroiditis following tirzepatide initiation that resolved after discontinuation.[11] The overall incidence of serious adverse events, acute pancreatitis, and cholelithiasis/cholecystitis across trials is extremely low (≤1%).[4]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1317:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2946:   13: This trial also reported significantly greater weight loss with all tirzepatide doses, with mean reductions of -7.6 kg, -9.3 kg, and -11.2 kg versus -5.7 kg with semaglutide.[12][2] A post hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found that a higher proportion of participants achieved stringent composite therapeutic targets (e.g., HbA1c <7.0% with ≥10% weight loss) with tirzepatide compared to semaglutide.[13] A comprehensive review noted the safety profiles of both agents are consistent with the incretin class, characterized predominantly by transient gastrointestinal events.[14] A network meta-analysis in patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin background therapy found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg provided statistically significant, greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight at the primary endpoint versus the GLP-1 receptor agonists dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[15]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1318:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2950:   17: In a phase 1 clinical trial specifically designed to assess islet function and insulin sensitivity, subcutaneous tirzepatide demonstrated effects on pancreatic islet function versus placebo or semaglutide in adults with type 2 diabetes.[16] GIP's role is nuanced; it stimulates glucose-dependent insulin secretion and, uniquely, also stimulates glucagon secretion during hyperglycemia, which one review suggests may subsequently enhance insulin secretion through intra-islet paracrine communication.[17] This contrasts with the historical view that GIP's glucagon stimulation was uniformly detrimental in type 2 diabetes.[17] In phase 2 trials, tirzepatide demonstrated dose-dependent improvements in beta-cell function, as measured by the Homeostatic Model Assessment 2 of beta-cell function (HOMA2-B) index.[18] The combined incretin action results in robust glycemic control, with one review of the SURPASS trials reporting that a greater proportion of participants receiving tirzepatide achieved HbA1c levels below 5.7% across baseline HbA1c categories compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[19]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1319:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2954:   21: A systematic review and meta-analysis of phase II/III trials confirmed that tirzepatide treatment resulted in a pooled mean HbA1c reduction of −1.94% and body weight reduction of −8.47 kg, with greater efficacy at higher doses.[20] Another meta-analysis reported similar incremental benefits: HbA1c was reduced by −0.20% with 10 mg and −0.30% with 15 mg versus 5 mg, and body weight was reduced by −2.64 kg with 10 mg and −4.26 kg with 15 mg versus 5 mg.[21] A Bayesian network meta-analysis ranked the 15 mg dose as having the highest probability (93.5%) of being the best option for HbA1c reduction and the highest probability (99.7%) for body weight reduction among active comparators.[3] The time to achieve glycemic targets also demonstrates a dose-response relationship; a pre-planned analysis of SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 found the median time to achieve HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all three tirzepatide doses, faster than with semaglutide 1 mg (12.0 weeks) or insulin degludec (12.1 weeks).[22] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal adverse events are the most common and show a dose-dependent increase in incidence.[23] Another analysis found the risk of adverse events was higher for the 10 mg (RR 1.20) and 15 mg (RR 1.26) doses compared to 5 mg.[21] Trial data corroborate this pattern; in SURPASS J-mono, the incidence of nausea was 12%, 20%, and 20% for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus 8% with dulaglutide 0.75 mg.[1] However, the incidence of serious adverse events and treatment discontinuation due to adverse events does not consistently show a clear dose-response trend across all trials.[23][21] A subgroup analysis of SURPASS-AP-Combo found the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events was generally similar across patient subgroups defined by baseline characteristics like age, BMI, and HbA1c for each tirzepatide dose.[24][25]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1321:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:2958:   25: Tirzepatide is approved under two distinct proprietary names in the United States: Mounjaro for type 2 diabetes mellitus and Zepbound for chronic weight management.[26][27] The US FDA approved Mounjaro (tirzepatide) injection on May 15, 2022, as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[26] Subsequently, the FDA approved Zepbound (tirzepatide) injection on November 8, 2023, as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for chronic weight management in adults with an initial body mass index (BMI) of 30 kg/m² or greater (obesity) or 27 kg/m² or greater (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbid condition.[27] In December 2024, the FDA expanded the Zepbound label to include treatment of moderate to severe obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) in adults with obesity.[28] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) granted a marketing authorization valid throughout the EU for Mounjaro on September 15, 2022, for the treatment of adults with insufficiently controlled type 2 diabetes as an adjunct to diet and exercise, either as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or as an add-on to other diabetes medicines.[29][30] The EMA also approved Mounjaro for weight management as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for weight loss and weight maintenance in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbidity.[31] The US prescribing information for both products carries a boxed warning for risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, stating that tirzepatide causes thyroid C-cell tumors in rats and that it is unknown whether it causes such tumors, including medullary thyroid carcinoma, in humans.[32][33] Consequently, both Mounjaro and Zepbound are contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or in patients with Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2).[32][33] The labels contain warnings and precautions for severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, acute kidney injury due to volume depletion, acute gallbladder disease, acute pancreatitis, hypersensitivity reactions, hypoglycemia with concomitant use of insulin secretagogues or insulin, diabetic retinopathy complications in patients with type 2 diabetes, and pulmonary aspiration during general anesthesia or deep sedation.[32][33] The Zepbound label also includes a specific warning to never share a Zepbound KwikPen between patients.[32] The Canadian Product Monograph similarly includes a Serious Warnings and Precautions box for thyroid C-cell tumors and contraindicates use in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN 2.[34] The recommended dosing regimen is consistent across indications and jurisdictions: therapy is initiated at 2.5 mg injected subcutaneously once weekly, with the dose increased in 2.5 mg increments after at least 4 weeks on the current dose based on treatment response and tolerability.[35][36][32][33] For glycemic control in type 2 diabetes, the maximum recommended dosage for adults is 15 mg once weekly, while for pediatric patients 10 years and older, the maximum is 10 mg once weekly.[33] For chronic weight management and obstructive sleep apnea, the recommended maintenance dosages are 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once weekly, with a maximum of 15 mg.[32] The drug product is supplied as a solution for injection in single-dose pens or vials across multiple strengths (2.5 mg/0.5 mL, 5 mg/0.5 mL, 7.5 mg/0.5 mL, 10 mg/0.5 mL, 12.5 mg/0.5 mL, 15 mg/0.5 mL).[32][33] The pharmacokinetics are well characterized, with an absolute bioavailability of approximately 80%, a time to maximum concentration ranging from 8 to 72 hours, and a terminal half-life of approximately 5 days supporting once-weekly administration.[35][36] In the UK, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommends tirzepatide (Mounjaro) for treating type 2 diabetes alongside diet and exercise in adults only under specific criteria: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral antidiabetic drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of at least 35 kg/m² with associated psychological or medical problems, or a BMI of less than 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy would have significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other significant obesity-related complications.[37][38] Lower BMI thresholds (usually reduced by 2.5 kg/m²) apply for people from South Asian, Chinese, other Asian, Middle Eastern, Black African, or African-Caribbean family backgrounds.[37] For weight management, NICE recommends tirzepatide (Zepbound) as an option for adults with an initial BMI of at least 35 kg/m² and at least one weight-related comorbidity, with the same ethnic BMI adjustments, and recommends assessing treatment response after 6 months on the highest tolerated dose.[39][40]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1463:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\findings.md:75:NICE is handled cleanly. .\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1487:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:134:"C:\\Program Files\\PowerShell\\7\\pwsh.exe" -Command 'Get-Content -Raw outputs/full_scale_v18/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json' in C:\POLARIS
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1488:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:140:This trial also reported that 82% to 86% of participants receiving tirzepatide achieved an HbA1c of less than 7.0% at 40 weeks, compared to 79% with semaglutide.[1] In the SURPASS-3 trial, a 52-week, open-label, active-controlled study comparing tirzepatide to insulin degludec in patients on metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor, HbA1c reductions were −1.93%, −2.20%, and −2.37% for tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg, respectively, versus −1.34% with insulin degludec.[2][3] A pre-planned exploratory analysis of SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 found that the median time to achieve an HbA1c of less than 7.0% was faster with tirzepatide (8.1 weeks for each dose) than with semaglutide 1 mg (12.0 weeks) or insulin degludec (12.1 weeks).[4] In the SURPASS-4 trial, which enrolled patients with type 2 diabetes and increased cardiovascular risk, tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg reduced HbA1c by −2.24%, −2.43%, and −2.58%, respectively, over 52 weeks, compared to a reduction of −1.44% with insulin glargine.[2][5] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial conducted in the Asia-Pacific region, all tirzepatide doses were superior to insulin glargine, with HbA1c reductions of −2.24%, −2.44%, and −2.49% for the 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus −0.95% with insulin glargine at 40 weeks.[2][6]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1489:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:144:Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most common safety concern with tirzepatide, occurring in a dose-dependent manner.[7] Another systematic review noted that compared to the 5 mg dose, tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg were associated with more frequent nausea (P<0.001) and vomiting (P=0.007 for 5 mg vs. 10 mg; P<0.001 for 5 mg vs. 15 mg).[8][9] These events often led to treatment discontinuation; one analysis found drug discontinuation due to adverse events was highest with the 15 mg dose at 10%, and another systematic review reported higher discontinuation rates with tirzepatide 10 mg (pooled RR=1.75) and 15 mg (pooled RR=2.03) compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[7][8][9] In the obesity trial SURMOUNT-4, during the initial open-label lead-in period with tirzepatide, 35.5% of participants experienced nausea, 21.1% diarrhea, 20.7% constipation, and 16.3% vomiting.[10][11] In SURPASS-AP-Combo, where tirzepatide was compared to insulin glargine, no severe hypoglycemia was reported.[6] A meta-analysis found the incidence of mild hypoglycemia (blood glucose <70 mg/dL) was highest with the tirzepatide 10 mg dose at 22.6% (95% CI, 9.2%-39.8%).[7] However, another systematic review concluded the overall safety profile was similar to GLP-1 receptor agonists except for a higher risk of hypoglycemia with the 15 mg dose (pooled RR=3.83, 95% CI 1.19-12.30) and higher discontinuation rates.[8][9] A meta-analysis found rates of MTC and other serious events like acute pancreatitis and cholecystitis were ≤1% across all tirzepatide doses.[7] In the SURMOUNT-CN obesity trial, one case of papillary thyroid cancer occurred in a participant with a history of thyroid nodules receiving tirzepatide 15 mg, which was considered unrelated to treatment by the investigator, and no cases of medullary thyroid cancer were reported.[12] Similarly, in the SURMOUNT-4 trial, no cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma were reported.[10][11] A real-world pharmacovigilance analysis identified a signal for medullary thyroid carcinoma with an EB05 of 3.55, based on 3 reported cases.[13] Other adverse events of interest include pancreatitis, with the same pharmacovigilance analysis reporting 190 cases and a signal strength (EB05=6.85), though adjudicated confirmed cases in trials were low; for example, SURMOUNT-4 reported no adjudication-confirmed cases of pancreatitis.[13][10][11] Gallbladder-related events such as cholelithiasis and acute cholecystitis have also been reported at low frequencies.[10][11][12][7]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1490:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:148:A systematic review and network meta-analysis of subcutaneous GLP-1 receptor agonists and tirzepatide concluded that all three tirzepatide doses were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c, while demonstrating greater weight loss than the corresponding semaglutide doses.[14] A post hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found tirzepatide increased the odds of achieving composite therapeutic targets, such as HbA1c <7.0% with weight loss >10%, compared to semaglutide 1 mg.[15] For weight loss specifically in populations with overweight or obesity, a meta-analysis of direct comparative studies reported a standardized mean difference of 0.75 favoring tirzepatide over semaglutide, and a real-world evidence study in a US cohort with overweight or obesity reported patients on tirzepatide had significantly higher rates of achieving ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15% weight loss within one year.[16][17] In SURPASS-3 versus insulin degludec, tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg produced greater HbA1c reductions and body weight reductions ranging from -7.5 kg to -12.9 kg, whereas insulin degludec was associated with weight gain of +2.3 kg.[3] Similarly, in SURPASS-4 versus insulin glargine in patients at increased cardiovascular risk, all tirzepatide doses provided superior HbA1c reductions and weight reductions between -6.4 kg and -10.6 kg, compared to weight gain of +1.9 kg with insulin glargine.[5] Another systematic review and meta-analysis confirmed these findings, reporting tirzepatide was associated with significantly greater proportions of patients achieving HbA1c targets of <7.0% (80-89% vs. 49%) and weight loss ≥5% (62-83% vs. 7%) compared to long-acting insulin.[18] A network meta-analysis in patients on basal insulin background therapy found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg provided statistically significant greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight versus GLP-1 receptor agonists dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[19] The indirect comparison versus semaglutide 2 mg reported a similar overall safety profile, though tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg had higher odds of treatment discontinuation due to adverse events.[20] Compared to basal insulin, tirzepatide is associated with more gastrointestinal adverse events but a significantly lower risk of hypoglycemia.[21][18]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1491:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:152:Tirzepatide demonstrates dose-dependent improvements in both glycemic control and body weight reduction across its 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses in patients with type 2 diabetes.[2][22][23] Similarly, for weight loss, the same meta-analysis found that compared to tirzepatide 5 mg, the 10 mg dose reduced body weight by an additional 2.64 kg and the 15 mg dose by an additional 4.26 kg.[23] This pattern is evident in individual trials; in SURPASS-3, weight reductions at 52 weeks were −7.5 kg, −10.7 kg, and −12.9 kg for the 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses, respectively, compared to a 2.3 kg increase with insulin degludec.[2][3] In SURPASS-4, weight changes were −7.1 kg, −9.5 kg, and −11.7 kg for the three doses versus a 1.9 kg increase with insulin glargine.[2][5] The proportion of patients achieving clinically meaningful weight loss thresholds also increases with dose.[2] The time to achieve treatment targets is also dose-influenced, with higher doses leading to faster responses.[4] A pre-planned analysis of SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 found that the median time to first achieve an HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all tirzepatide doses, compared to 12.0 weeks for semaglutide 1 mg and 12.1 weeks for insulin degludec.[4] For weight loss, the median time to achieve ≥5% body weight reduction in SURPASS-2 was 16.0 weeks with tirzepatide 5 mg and 12.4 weeks with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses, versus 24.0 weeks with semaglutide 1 mg.[4] While efficacy increases with dose, so does the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events, though serious adverse event rates are generally similar across doses.[22][23] One meta-analysis reports that the risk of total adverse events was significantly higher with tirzepatide 10 mg (risk ratio 1.20) and 15 mg (risk ratio 1.26) compared to the 5 mg dose.[23] Another analysis concluded that tirzepatide 5 mg had the best safety profile among the three doses, while the 15 mg dose provided the greatest efficacy benefit for glycemic and weight control.[22] For optimal long-term weight maintenance, continued treatment with the maximum tolerated dose (10 mg or 15 mg) is necessary, as evidenced by the SURMOUNT-4 trial where switching to placebo after 36 weeks of tirzepatide led to a 14.0% weight regain, while continued treatment yielded an additional 5.5% reduction.[11]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1493:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:156:Tirzepatide is approved by the FDA under the proprietary name Mounjaro as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[24] The FDA-approved dosing regimen starts at 2.5 mg subcutaneously once weekly, with increases in 2.5 mg increments after a minimum of 4 weeks on the current dose, up to a maximum of 15 mg once weekly.[24][25] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) authorizes tirzepatide (Mounjaro) for the same T2DM indication in adults, as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or as an add-on to other antidiabetic medicines.[26][27] The EMA also approves tirzepatide for weight management as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbidity.[28][26] For the chronic weight management indication, the FDA approved tirzepatide under the name Zepbound, with an identical starting and titration schedule up to 15 mg once weekly.[29][30] A key safety warning from both agencies is a boxed warning for risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, as tirzepatide caused thyroid C-cell tumors in rats, though human relevance is unknown.[31][32] Consequently, tirzepatide is contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) or in patients with Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2).[31][32] Other warnings and precautions common to the labels include the risk of severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, acute pancreatitis, hypoglycemia with concomitant insulin secretagogues or insulin, hypersensitivity reactions, acute kidney injury, acute gallbladder disease, and potential impact on absorption of oral medications due to delayed gastric emptying.[31][32] The EMA product information additionally notes a mean increase in heart rate and includes diabetic retinopathy complications and pulmonary aspiration during anesthesia as precautions.[26] The UK's National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommends tirzepatide for treating T2DM in adults only under specific conditions: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral antidiabetic drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of 35 kg/m² or more with associated psychological or medical problems, or a BMI under 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy has significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other obesity-related complications.[33][34] NICE guidance also specifies using lower BMI thresholds for people from certain ethnic backgrounds.[33] For weight management, NICE provides a prescribing guide, confirming the starting dose of 2.5 mg once weekly and titration by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks to recommended maintenance doses of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once weekly.[35]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1706:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:3034:Claim architecture: A typical V17 claim is compressed into: source type or trial name -> broad conclusion -> citation. It often omits design, N, baseline, endpoint, dose-specific absolute result, comparator result, confidence interval, and p-value in the same sentence. Example: "In SURPASS-3 versus insulin degludec, tirzepatide also demonstrated superior glycemic control and weight loss..." (line 5). That is directionally correct, but it does not carry the trial skeleton that lets a reader judge magnitude or applicability.
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1716:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:3076:Contradiction handling: Gemini handles some source differences poorly. That is an internal contradiction: the reported endpoint supports noninferiority with numerical trends, not definitive cardiovascular superiority.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2175:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:293:In the pivotal SURPASS phase 3 trials, all doses of tirzepatide demonstrated superiority in reducing A1C and body weight from baseline versus placebo or active comparators in adults with type 2 diabetes.[1] The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] In SURPASS-2, which compared tirzepatide to semaglutide 1 mg, reductions in A1C at week 40 were −2.09%, −2.37%, and −2.46% for tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg, respectively, versus −1.86% for semaglutide.[1] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the completed SURPASS trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] Body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine in SURPASS-5 to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor in SURPASS-3.[1] In SURPASS-6, adding tirzepatide to insulin glargine resulted in an estimated mean change from baseline in HbA1c of −2.1% versus −1.1% with added prandial insulin lispro at week 52.[2] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial in the Asia-Pacific region, all tirzepatide doses were superior to insulin glargine for HbA1c reduction, with reductions of −2.24%, −2.44%, and −2.49% for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus −0.95% for insulin glargine at week 40.[3] In the same trial, body weight reductions were −5.0 kg, −7.0 kg, and −7.2 kg for the respective tirzepatide doses compared to a 1.5 kg increase with insulin glargine.[3]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2176:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:305:A network meta-analysis found that compared to the 5 mg dose, tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg provided significantly greater reductions in HbA1c (mean differences of -0.19% and -0.32%, respectively) and body weight (mean differences of -1.96 kg and -3.31 kg, respectively).[11] A separate analysis confirmed that tirzepatide 15 mg significantly reduced HbA1c by 0.30% and weight by 4.26 kg compared to the 5 mg dose.[12] This dose-response pattern for weight loss is also observed in a related obesity trial without diabetes, where higher tirzepatide doses (10 mg and 15 mg) were associated with higher proportions of participants achieving various weight reduction thresholds and greater mean percent weight reductions at 72 weeks compared to the 5 mg dose.[13] Regarding the time to achieve treatment goals, a pre-planned analysis of SURPASS trials reported that the median time to reach an HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all three tirzepatide doses, while the median time to first achieve ≥5% weight loss was 16.0 weeks for the 5 mg dose and 12.4 weeks for the 10 and 15 mg doses.[14] However, the increased efficacy of higher doses is accompanied by a higher risk of adverse events, particularly gastrointestinal events, with one analysis noting a significantly higher risk of any adverse event for the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[12] Consequently, one review concluded that while tirzepatide 15 mg provides the best efficacy for glycemic and weight reduction, the 5 mg dose offers a more favorable safety profile.[15]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2177:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:309:Tirzepatide is a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist.[16] A phase 1 clinical trial specifically assessed the effects of subcutaneous tirzepatide versus placebo or semaglutide on pancreatic islet function and insulin sensitivity in adults with type 2 diabetes.[17] A systematic review describes that the dual incretin agonism of tirzepatide enhances insulin secretion in a glucose-dependent manner and improves insulin sensitivity.[18] In a substudy of the SURPASS-3 trial, tirzepatide was shown to reduce liver fat content and abdominal adipose tissue in people with type 2 diabetes compared with insulin degludec.[19] The combined infusion of GIP and GLP-1 is noted to produce a more pronounced glucose-lowering effect and enhanced insulin secretion than the administration of either agent alone.[16] The reduction in liver fat and visceral adipose tissue is a mechanism by which tirzepatide may improve systemic insulin sensitivity.[19]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2188:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:511:"C:\\Program Files\\PowerShell\\7\\pwsh.exe" -Command 'if (Test-Path outputs/full_scale_v5/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json) { Get-Content -Raw outputs/full_scale_v5/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json } else { Get-ChildItem outputs -Recurse -Filter bibliography.json | Where-Object {$_.FullName -match '"'tirzepatide'} | Select-Object FullName }" in C:\POLARIS
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2196:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:745:Tirzepatide demonstrated significant, dose-dependent improvements in both glycemic control and body weight across the SURPASS clinical trials in adults with type 2 diabetes.[1] The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] In SURPASS-1, the highest dose (15 mg) reduced A1C by 2.07% from baseline and 52% of participants achieved normoglycemia (A1c <5.7%).[1][2] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] For weight loss, body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine (SURPASS-5) to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor (SURPASS-3).[1] Tirzepatide was superior to active comparators, including semaglutide 1 mg and various insulin regimens, in reducing A1C and body weight.[1][3] These effects were consistent across different patient populations and background therapies.[1][4]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2197:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:753:Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most commonly reported adverse events with tirzepatide and are dose-dependent, with reported incidences of 39% (95% CI, 35%-43%), 46% (95% CI, 42%-49%), and 49% (95% CI, 38%-60%) for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively.[15] Among gastrointestinal events, nausea and diarrhea are the most frequent at any dose.[15] Drug discontinuation due to adverse events was highest with the 15 mg dose at 10%.[15] Regarding hypoglycemia, a meta-analysis found patients treated with tirzepatide had no significantly increased risk compared with placebo overall (OR= 2.05, 95%CI:0.95 to 4.42, p=0.07).[16] Another analysis reported tirzepatide 15mg was associated with more hypoglycemia than GLP-1 receptor agonists (pooled RR=3.83, 95% CI [1.19- 12.30], P=0.02).[17] Conversely, a different study in patients achieving normoglycemia found low rates, with 98.5% not experiencing level 2 or 3 severe hypoglycemia (<54 mg/dL) and 91% not experiencing level 1 hypoglycemia (≤70 mg/dL).[18] Rates of fatal adverse events, severe hypoglycemia, acute pancreatitis, cholelithiasis, and cholecystitis are reported as extremely low (≤ 1%) across all doses.[15] An analysis of FDA adverse event reports suggested an association between tirzepatide and thyroid cancer (reporting odds ratio = 2.09, 95% CI: 1.51–2.89), though this was lower than associations for other GLP-1 receptor agonists.[19]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2198:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:761:In a post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-1 to -5, tirzepatide demonstrated significantly greater reductions in HbA1c versus comparators in both prespecified age subgroups (<65 and ≥65 years).[23] Regarding sex, all tirzepatide doses significantly reduced body weight compared to comparators regardless of the sex of study participants across SURPASS-1 to -5.[24] In patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and obesity, tirzepatide produced less weight loss at 52 weeks in those with type 2 diabetes (10.4%) than in those without diabetes (12.9%).[25] However, the effect of tirzepatide on the primary composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure was similar between patients with or without diabetes, with a hazard ratio of 0.64 for patients with diabetes and 0.61 for those without.[25] In a retrospective study of adults with type 1 diabetes and obesity, tirzepatide led to significant weight loss, with a median total body weight loss of 8% at 6 months and a 1% reduction in HbA1c.[26] For individuals with type 2 diabetes who were glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibody-positive, tirzepatide was effective, achieving HbA1c reductions of -2.11% and body weight reductions of -9.2 kg at 40/42 weeks, though HbA1c reductions were slightly greater in GADA-negative patients.[27] The most frequent adverse events across subgroups were mild-to-moderate and gastrointestinal-related, occurring during dose-escalation.[23][24]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2260:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1358:outputs/full_scale_v5/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:21:In a post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-1 to -5, tirzepatide demonstrated significantly greater reductions in HbA1c versus comparators in both prespecified age subgroups (<65 and ≥65 years).[23] Regarding sex, all tirzepatide doses significantly reduced body weight compared to comparators regardless of the sex of study participants across SURPASS-1 to -5.[24] In patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and obesity, tirzepatide produced less weight loss at 52 weeks in those with type 2 diabetes (10.4%) than in those without diabetes (12.9%).[25] However, the effect of tirzepatide on the primary composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure was similar between patients with or without diabetes, with a hazard ratio of 0.64 for patients with diabetes and 0.61 for those without.[25] In a retrospective study of adults with type 1 diabetes and obesity, tirzepatide led to significant weight loss, with a median total body weight loss of 8% at 6 months and a 1% reduction in HbA1c.[26] For individuals with type 2 diabetes who were glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibody-positive, tirzepatide was effective, achieving HbA1c reductions of -2.11% and body weight reductions of -9.2 kg at 40/42 weeks, though HbA1c reductions were slightly greater in GADA-negative patients.[27] The most frequent adverse events across subgroups were mild-to-moderate and gastrointestinal-related, occurring during dose-escalation.[23][24]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2265:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1365:outputs/full_scale_v5/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json:12:    "statement": "100-OR: ADA Presidents\u2019 Select Abstract: Efficacy and Safety of Once-Weekly Tirzepatide, a Dual GIP/GLP-1 Receptor Agonist vs. Placebo as Monotherapy in People with Type 2 Diabetes (SURPASS-1)",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2266:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1366:outputs/full_scale_v5/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json:21:    "url": "https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/tirzepatide-significantly-reduced-a1c-and-body-weight-in-people-with-type-2-diabetes-in-two-phase-3-trials-from-lillys-surpass-program-301229506.html"
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2267:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1367:outputs/full_scale_v5/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json:26:    "statement": "227-OR: Tirzepatide Reduces HbA1c and Body Weight Significantly More than Placebo Regardless of Insulin Sensitivity and Beta-Cell Function\u2014Post Hoc Analysis from SURMOUNT-2",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2268:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1368:outputs/full_scale_v5/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/bibliography.json:82:    "statement": "Tirzepatide vs Insulin Lispro Added to Basal Insulin in Type 2 Diabetes: The SURPASS-6 Randomized Clinical Trial.",
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2269:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1369:outputs/full_scale_v6/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:5:In the pivotal SURPASS phase 3 trials, all doses of tirzepatide demonstrated superiority in reducing A1C and body weight from baseline versus placebo or active comparators in adults with type 2 diabetes.[1] The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] In SURPASS-2, which compared tirzepatide to semaglutide 1 mg, reductions in A1C at week 40 were −2.09%, −2.37%, and −2.46% for tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg, respectively, versus −1.86% for semaglutide.[1] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the completed SURPASS trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] Body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine in SURPASS-5 to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor in SURPASS-3.[1] In SURPASS-6, adding tirzepatide to insulin glargine resulted in an estimated mean change from baseline in HbA1c of −2.1% versus −1.1% with added prandial insulin lispro at week 52.[2] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial in the Asia-Pacific region, all tirzepatide doses were superior to insulin glargine for HbA1c reduction, with reductions of −2.24%, −2.44%, and −2.49% for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus −0.95% for insulin glargine at week 40.[3] In the same trial, body weight reductions were −5.0 kg, −7.0 kg, and −7.2 kg for the respective tirzepatide doses compared to a 1.5 kg increase with insulin glargine.[3]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:2270:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_3\codex_stderr.log:1370:outputs/full_scale_v6/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:21:Tirzepatide is a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist.[16] A phase 1 clinical trial specifically assessed the effects of subcutaneous tirzepatide versus placebo or semaglutide on pancreatic islet function and insulin sensitivity in adults with type 2 diabetes.[17] A systematic review describes that the dual incretin agonism of tirzepatide enhances insulin secretion in a glucose-dependent manner and improves insulin sensitivity.[18] In a substudy of the SURPASS-3 trial, tirzepatide was shown to reduce liver fat content and abdominal adipose tissue in people with type 2 diabetes compared with insulin degludec.[19] The combined infusion of GIP and GLP-1 is noted to produce a more pronounced glucose-lowering effect and enhanced insulin secretion than the administration of either agent alone.[16] The reduction in liver fat and visceral adipose tissue is a mechanism by which tirzepatide may improve systemic insulin sensitivity.[19]
